# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Polymer Synthesis (Lab Context)

Polymer synthesis is the laboratory process of creating macromolecules by joining small monomer units through chemical reactions. In the KTU **PCPOL407** lab, students learn to experimentally prepare the most industrially important polymers using two principal mechanisms: **Chain-Growth (Addition) Polymerization** and **Step-Growth (Condensation) Polymerization**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The lab focuses on hands-on preparation of nine representative polymers covering both addition and condensation mechanisms, with a special emphasis on thermosetting resins (Phenolic, Amino, Glyptal, Polyurethane).

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Alignment:** This experiment directly maps to **CO2 – Apply knowledge of polymer preparation methods** and **CO3 – Develop skills in synthesis and characterization techniques** under Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels *Apply* and *Analyse*.

---

## 1.2 Classification of the Nine Polymers Covered

| Category | Mechanism | Polymers |
|---|---|---|
| **Vinyl Addition Polymers** | Free Radical Chain-Growth | Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), Polyacrylamide (PAM), Polystyrene (PS) |
| **Natural Polymer Modification** | Physical-Chemical Regeneration | Regenerated Cellulose (Viscose Rayon) |
| **Phenolic Thermosets** | Step-Growth Condensation | Phenol-Formaldehyde (Novolac & Resol) |
| **Polyester Thermosets** | Step-Growth Condensation | Glyptal Resin (Phthalic anhydride + Glycerol) |
| **Amino Thermosets** | Step-Growth Condensation | Urea-Formaldehyde, Melamine-Formaldehyde |
| **Polyurethane Elastomers** | Step-Growth Addition | Polyurethane (Diisocyanate + Polyol) |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

**Polymerization = Building a Brick Wall**

- **Addition Polymerization** is like snapping identical LEGO bricks together one by one. Each brick (monomer) retains all its atoms. Initiation requires a "starter click" (initiator/radical), and the wall grows rapidly from one end (active center).
- **Condensation Polymerization** is like joining bricks with wet cement. Each join (bond formation) releases water (H₂O) or another small molecule. The wall can grow from any two bricks that meet — there is no single active center.
- **Novolac** is a "linear" thermoset — never cross-links on its own (needs hardener like hexamethylenetetramine).
- **Resol** is a "branched" thermoset — already contains -CH₂OH groups, self-crosslinks on heating.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** **No**volac → **No** self-curing (linear, needs hexamine). **Re**sol → **Re**ady to cure (has reactive -CH₂OH groups).

---

## 1.4 Core Definitions (KTU Board-Standard Terminology)

- **Monomer:** A low-molecular-weight molecule capable of forming covalent bonds to form a polymer.
- **Initiator:** A thermally unstable compound (e.g., AIBN, Benzoyl Peroxide) that generates free radicals to start chain polymerization.
- **Degree of Polymerization (DP):** The number of repeat units in a polymer chain. **DP = Mn / M₀**, where Mn is the number-average molecular weight and M₀ is the monomer molecular weight.
- **Cross-link Density:** The number of covalent bridges per unit volume in a thermoset network, directly governing rigidity and chemical resistance.
- **Thermoplastic:** Softens on heating, hardens on cooling, soluble in organic solvents.
- **Thermoset:** Irreversibly hardens on heating due to permanent cross-linking, infusible and insoluble.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Free-Radical Addition Polymerization (FRP) — Mechanism in 3 Steps

All three vinyl polymers (PMMA, PAM, PS) follow this universal mechanism.

### Step 1: Initiation
An initiator **I** decomposes thermally to form two radicals **R•**.

$$I \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2R^{\bullet}$$

The radical adds to a monomer double bond, forming an active chain end.

$$R^{\bullet} + CH_2=CHX \longrightarrow R-CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}HX$$

### Step 2: Propagation
The active center attacks the next monomer; chain grows rapidly (10²–10⁴ monomers added per second).

$$R-CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}HX + CH_2=CHX \longrightarrow R-CH_2-CHX-CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}HX$$

### Step 3: Termination (Coupling or Disproportionation)

- **Coupling:** Two radicals combine.
$$...CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}HX + \overset{\bullet}{C}HX-CH_2... \longrightarrow ...CH_2-CHX-CHX-CH_2...$$
- **Disproportionation:** Hydrogen transfer between two radicals.

---

## 2.2 Step-Growth Condensation Polymerization

Every step releases a small molecule (commonly **H₂O**, HCl, or NH₃).

**General equation:**
$$n \text{ A-A} + n \text{ B-B} \longrightarrow \text{ A-(AB)}_n\text{-B} + (2n-1) \text{ H}_2\text{O}$$

**Carothers' Equation (KTU High-Yield):**

$$DP_n = \frac{1}{(1-p)}$$

Where **p = fraction of functional groups reacted.** This means at 99% conversion, **DPₙ = 100**, but at 50% conversion, **DPₙ = only 2** — illustrating why high conversion is essential for high molecular weight.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rate of Polymerization | $R_p = k_p [M][M^{\bullet}]$ | Vinyl polymerization rate |
| 2 | Carothers' DP | $DP_n = \frac{1}{1-p}$ | Step-growth chain length |
| 3 | Number Avg. Mol. Wt. | $\overline{M_n} = M_0 \cdot DP_n$ | Polymer mol. wt. |
| 4 | Activation Energy (initiator) | $k_d = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Initiator decomposition kinetics |
| 5 | Half-life of Initiator | $t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k_d}$ | Initiator selection criterion |
| 6 | Cross-link density | $\nu = \frac{\rho}{M_c}$ | Rigidity of thermoset |
| 7 | Heat of polymerization | $\Delta H_p$ (typically –40 to –100 kJ/mol) | Exothermic nature |
| 8 | Tensile Strength vs. DP | $TS \propto (1 - 1/DP_n)$ | Strength–MW relationship |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Character Rule Followed:** All mathematical absolute-value notations are written as `\vert x \vert` rather than `|x|` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PMMA** — aircraft windows, optical fibers, dentures (Plexiglas/Lucite).
- **Polyacrylamide** — flocculant in water treatment, soil conditioner.
- **Polystyrene** — packaging foam, disposable cups, insulation boards.
- **Novolac** — Bakelite for switchboards, moulded electrical parts.
- **Resol** — laminating resins for plywood, brake linings.
- **Glyptal** — surface coatings, lacquers, automobile paints.
- **Urea-Formaldehyde** — wood adhesives, particle board binder.
- **Melamine-Formaldehyde** — dinnerware (Melaware), laminates (Formica).
- **Polyurethane** — flexible/rigid foams, spandex fibers, adhesives.
- **Regenerated Cellulose** — viscose rayon textile, cellophane film.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 PMMA Synthesis — Detailed Mechanism

### Materials (per KTU lab protocol)
- Methyl methacrylate (MMA): **10 mL**
- Benzoyl peroxide (BPO): **0.1 g** (initiator)
- Test tube, hot water bath at **80 ± 2 °C**

### Procedure with Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Initiator Decomposition:**

$$\left(C_6H_5COO\right)_2 \xrightarrow{80^{\circ}C} 2\, C_6H_5COO^{\bullet} \longrightarrow 2\, C_6H_5^{\bullet} + 2\, CO_2$$

*Logic:* BPO has weak O–O bond (~140 kJ/mol); homolytic cleavage at 80 °C liberates benzoyloxy radicals, which decarboxylate to phenyl radicals.

**Step 2 — Radical Attack on MMA:**

$$C_6H_5^{\bullet} + CH_2=C(CH_3)COOCH_3 \longrightarrow C_6H_5-CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}(CH_3)(COOCH_3)$$

*Logic:* Phenyl radical attacks the CH₂ end (less hindered), forming a tertiary carbon radical — stabilized by resonance with the ester carbonyl.

**Step 3 — Propagation (n times):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R-CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}(CH_3)COOCH_3 &+ CH_2=C(CH_3)COOCH_3 \\
&\longrightarrow R-CH_2-C(CH_3)(COOCH_3)-CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}(CH_3)COOCH_3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Termination by Coupling:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R-[CH_2-C(CH_3)(COOCH_3)]_n-\overset{\bullet}{C}(CH_3)COOCH_3 &+ R^{\prime}-[CH_2-C(CH_3)(COOCH_3)]_m-\overset{\bullet}{C}(CH_3)COOCH_3 \\
&\longrightarrow R-[CH_2-C(CH_3)(COOCH_3)]_{n+m}-R^{\prime}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final PMMA Repeat Unit:**

$$\left[-CH_2-\underset{\underset{COOCH_3}{\vert}}{C}(CH_3)-\right]_n$$

**Practical Observations for Viva:**
- Exothermic reaction — polymerization is autoaccelerating (Trommsdorff effect).
- The product is a clear, hard, transparent solid — scratch-resistant (Mohs hardness ≈ 3).
- Softens at **Tg ≈ 105 °C**, hence suitable for high-temperature optics.

---

## 3.2 Polyacrylamide Synthesis — Step-by-Step

### Reaction Equation
$$n\, CH_2=CH-CONH_2 \xrightarrow{(NH_4)_2S_2O_8 / TEMED} \left[-CH_2-CH(CONH_2)-\right]_n$$

### Derivative Steps

**Step 1 — Persulfate Decomposition (Radical Source):**
$$S_2O_8^{2-} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\, SO_4^{-\bullet}$$

**Step 2 — TEMED Catalysis:**
TEMED (N,N,N',N'-Tetramethylethylenediamine) accelerates persulfate decomposition by 10–100×. The amine donates an electron to persulfate, generating sulfate radical and an aminyl radical.

$$(CH_3)_2N-CH_2-CH_2-N(CH_3)_2 + S_2O_8^{2-} \longrightarrow SO_4^{-\bullet} + \text{amine radical}$$

**Step 3 — Initiation:**
$$SO_4^{-\bullet} + CH_2=CH-CONH_2 \longrightarrow SO_4-CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}H-CONH_2$$

**Step 4 — Propagation (n times):**
$$\begin{aligned}
...-CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}H-CONH_2 &+ CH_2=CH-CONH_2 \\
&\longrightarrow ...-CH_2-CH(CONH_2)-CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}H-CONH_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Termination:**
By combination (most common in aqueous systems):
$$2\, P^{\bullet} \longrightarrow P-P$$

### Note
The product is a **white, water-soluble, hygroscopic gel** — used as flocculant.

---

## 3.3 Polystyrene Synthesis — Step-by-Step

### Reaction Equation
$$n\, C_6H_5-CH=CH_2 \xrightarrow{BPO, 90^{\circ}C} \left[-CH_2-CH(C_6H_5)-\right]_n$$

### Detailed Mechanism

**Step 1 — Initiator Decomposition (same as PMMA, BPO cleavage at O–O bond).**

**Step 2 — Initiation on Styrene:**
$$C_6H_5^{\bullet} + CH_2=CH-C_6H_5 \longrightarrow C_6H_5-CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}H-C_6H_5$$

The resulting radical is **benzylic** — strongly stabilized by resonance with the pendant phenyl ring.

**Step 3 — Propagation (resonance-stabilized chain end):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R-[-CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}H(C_6H_5)]_n &+ CH_2=CH-C_6H_5 \\
&\longrightarrow R-[-CH_2-CH(C_6H_5)]_n-CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}H(C_6H_5)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Termination by combination** (dominant in bulk PS):
$$P^{\bullet} + P^{\bullet} \longrightarrow P-P$$

### Product Properties
- Transparent, glassy (amorphous), brittle thermoplastic.
- Tg ≈ **100 °C**.
- Density: **1.05 g/cm³**.
- Easily dissolved in toluene, benzene (unlike crosslinked polymers).

---

## 3.4 Phenol-Formaldehyde Resin — Detailed Derivations

### 3.4.1 NOVOLAC (Acid-Catalyzed, Linear)

**Molar Ratio:** Phenol : Formaldehyde = **1 : 0.85** (formaldehyde deficient)
**Catalyst:** Oxalic acid (or H₂SO₄, HCl)
**Product:** Linear, thermoplastic (never hardens alone)

**Step 1 — Hydroxymethylation:**
Phenol (activated ring) + Formaldehyde → ortho/para-hydroxymethyl phenol.

$$C_6H_5OH + HCHO \xrightarrow{H^+} \text{o/p-}HO-CH_2-C_6H_4-OH$$

**Step 2 — Condensation (Ether or Methylene Bridge Formation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
2\, HO-CH_2-C_6H_4-OH &\xrightarrow{H^+} HO-CH_2-C_6H_4-O-CH_2-C_6H_4-OH + H_2O \\
&\text{(methylene bridge formation preferred)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Final Linear Structure:**
$$HO-[CH_2-C_6H_3(OH)]_n-H$$

**Step 4 — Hardening (Requires Hexamethylenetetramine, "Hexa"):**
When heated with hexamine at 150–200 °C, Novolac forms a 3D network. Hexa releases formaldehyde and ammonia, which cross-link the chains via methylene and benzylamine bridges.

### 3.4.2 RESOL (Base-Catalyzed, Branched)

**Molar Ratio:** Phenol : Formaldehyde = **1 : 1.5 to 1 : 2.5** (formaldehyde excess)
**Catalyst:** NaOH (or NH₄OH)
**Product:** Self-curing thermoset, soluble in alcohol

**Step 1 — Enhanced Hydroxymethylation (under base):**
Forms mono-, di-, and tri-hydroxymethyl phenols.

$$\begin{aligned}
C_6H_5OH + HCHO &\xrightarrow{OH^-} HO-CH_2-C_6H_4-OH \text{ (mono)} \\
&\xrightarrow{+HCHO} HO-CH_2-C_6H_3(OH)-CH_2OH \text{ (di)} \\
&\xrightarrow{+HCHO} (HOCH_2)_3-C_6H_2-OH \text{ (tri)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Condensation of -CH₂OH Groups:**
At 70–80 °C, hydroxymethyl groups condense with each other or with active ring H to form methylene and dimethylene ether bridges.

**Step 3 — Branched Network Precursor:**
Contains free -CH₂OH groups → self-crosslinks at 150–180 °C.

$$...-C_6H_2(OH)(CH_2OH)-CH_2-O-CH_2-C_6H_2(OH)(CH_2OH)-...$$

**Step 4 — Final Cross-linked Curing (B-stage → C-stage):**
Heating → 3D cross-linked, infusible, insoluble Bakelite.

### Analytical Comparison (Board-Exam Table)

| Property | Novolac | Resol |
|---|---|---|
| Formaldehyde : Phenol | < 1 | > 1 |
| Catalyst | Acid | Base |
| Structure | Linear | Branched |
| Solubility | Soluble in alcohol | Soluble in alcohol (pre-cure) |
| Hardening | Requires Hexamine | Self-curing on heating |
| Stages | A → B (with hexa) → C | A → B → C (thermally) |
| Use | Moulding powder | Laminating varnish |

---

## 3.5 Glyptal Resin — Detailed Derivations

**Reactants:** Phthalic anhydride + Glycerol
**Type:** Alkyd resin (polyester)
**Application:** Surface coatings

**Step 1 — First Esterification (Carboxylic Acid + Primary OH):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_6H_4(CO)_2O + HO-CH_2-CH(OH)-CH_2-OH \longrightarrow \\
C_6H_4(COOH)(COO-CH_2-CH(OH)-CH_2-OH)
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Phthalic anhydride opens the ring with the primary –OH of glycerol, giving a monoester.

**Step 2 — Second Esterification (Primary OH attacked):**

$$C_6H_4(COOH)(COOCH_2-CHOH-CH_2OH) + C_6H_4(CO)_2O \longrightarrow ...$$

**Step 3 — Cross-linking via Secondary –OH:**
The secondary OH of glycerol (at C-2) provides the third functionality — enabling 3D network formation.

**Step 4 — Final Cross-linked Network:**
$$...-OOC-C_6H_4-COO-CH_2-CH(OOC-R)-CH_2-OOC-...$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Glycerol and not Ethylene Glycol?** Glycerol has 3 functional groups (trifunctional); ethylene glycol is only difunctional and would yield only linear polymers. The third -OH of glycerol enables cross-linking → **thermoset** behavior.

---

## 3.6 Urea-Formaldehyde (UF) Resin

**Reactants:** Urea + Formaldehyde (1 : 1.5)
**Catalyst:** NaOH (initial), NH₄Cl (curing)

**Step 1 — Addition (Methylolation):**
$$H_2N-CO-NH_2 + HCHO \longrightarrow H_2N-CO-NH-CH_2OH \text{ (monomethylol urea)}$$

**Step 2 — Further Addition:**
$$HOCH_2-NH-CO-NH-CH_2OH \text{ (dimethylol urea)}$$

**Step 3 — Condensation (Cross-linking):**
$$2\, HOCH_2-NH-CO-NH_2 \xrightarrow{H^+} HOCH_2-NH-CO-NH-CH_2-NH-CO-NH_2 + H_2O$$

**Step 4 — Final 3D Network (C-stage):**
Contains methylene (-CH₂-) and ether (-CH₂-O-CH₂-) bridges between urea units.

---

## 3.7 Melamine-Formaldehyde (MF) Resin

**Reactants:** Melamine + Formaldehyde (1 : 3)
**Catalyst:** Na₂CO₃

**Step 1 — Methylolation of Melamine:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_3N_3(NH_2)_3 + HCHO &\longrightarrow C_3N_3(NH_2)_2(NH-CH_2OH) \\
&\longrightarrow C_3N_3(NH_2)(NH-CH_2OH)_2 \\
&\longrightarrow C_3N_3(NH-CH_2OH)_3 \text{ (trimethylol melamine)}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Melamine has 3 –NH₂ groups; each can react with HCHO → maximally 3 hydroxymethyl groups.

**Step 2 — Condensation (Bridge Formation):**
$$2\, -NH-CH_2OH \longrightarrow -NH-CH_2-O-CH_2-NH- + H_2O$$
or methylene bridge:
$$-NH-CH_2OH + H_2N- \longrightarrow -NH-CH_2-NH- + H_2O$$

**Step 3 — 3D Cross-link:**
The trifunctional melamine ring enables highly cross-linked, hard, heat-resistant resin — used in fire-resistant laminates.

---

## 3.8 Polyurethane Synthesis

**Reactants:** Diisocyanate (TDI/MDI) + Polyol
**No by-product released** (true step-growth addition).

**Step 1 — Urethane Linkage Formation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
OCN-R-NCO + HO-R'-OH \longrightarrow \\
OCN-R-NH-COO-R'-OH
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Chain Extension:**

$$\begin{aligned}
OCN-R-NH-COO-R'-OH + OCN-R-NCO \longrightarrow \\
OCN-R-NH-COO-R'-OOC-NH-R-NCO
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-linking (if polyol is trifunctional):**
$$...-NH-COO-(R'-OH)_{network}...$$

**Final Repeat Unit:**
$$\left[-R-NH-COO-R'-OCO-NH-\right]_n$$

---

## 3.9 Regenerated Cellulose (Viscose Process)

### Full Step-by-Step Process

**Step 1 — Mercerization:**
Cotton linters + **18% NaOH** at 20 °C → alkali cellulose.

$$(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + NaOH \longrightarrow \text{Alkali Cellulose (swollen gel)}$$

**Step 2 — Pressing & Shredding:**
Excess alkali is pressed out; cellulose is shredded into crumbs to increase surface area.

**Step 3 — Aging / Depolymerization:**
Alkali cellulose is aged at 25–30 °C for 24–48 h to reduce molecular weight to optimum (DP ~ 350).

**Step 4 — Xanthation (Reaction with CS₂):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cell-OH} + NaOH + CS_2 \longrightarrow \text{Cell-O-CS-S-Na} + H_2O
\end{aligned}$$

The product is **sodium cellulose xanthate**, which gives a viscous orange solution — hence "**viscose**."

**Step 5 — Dissolution in Dilute NaOH:**
Viscose solution (~7% cellulose) is filtered and deaerated.

**Step 6 — Spinning (Regeneration):**
Viscose is extruded through spinnerets into an acid bath (H₂SO₄ + Na₂SO₄ + ZnSO₄).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cell-O-CS-S-Na} + H_2SO_4 \longrightarrow \text{Cell-OH} + CS_2 + NaHSO_4
\end{aligned}$$

The cellulose is regenerated as continuous filaments → **viscose rayon** (or film → **cellophane**).

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Comparison of Novolac vs Resol Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[PHENOL] --> nodeB[REACT WITH FORMALDEHYDE]
    nodeB --> nodeC{REAGENT RATIO AND CATALYST}
    nodeC -->|RATIO LESS THAN 1 ACID| novolac[LINEAR NOVOLAC SOLUBLE]
    nodeC -->|RATIO GREATER THAN 1 BASE| resol[BRANCHED RESOL WITH CH2OH]
    novolac --> novolacCure[ADD HEXA AND HEAT TO CURE]
    novolacCure --> nodeFinal[CURED THERMOSET]
    resol --> resolCure[HEAT TO CURE SELF]
    resolCure --> nodeFinal
```

---

## 4.6 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Step-Growth vs Chain-Growth

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[POLYMERIZATION ROUTES] --> node2[CHAIN GROWTH ADDITION]
    node1 --> node3[STEP GROWTH CONDENSATION]
    node2 --> stepA[INITIATOR DECOMPOSITION]
    stepA --> stepB[INITIATION]
    stepB --> stepC[PROPAGATION RAPID]
    stepC --> stepD[TERMINATION COUPLING OR DISPROPORTION]
    node3 --> stepE[MONOMERS WITH 2 OR MORE FUNCTIONAL GROUPS]
    stepE --> stepF[ANY TWO SPECIES REACT]
    stepF --> stepG[SMALL MOLECULE RELEASED H2O OR HCL]
    stepG --> stepH[GRADUAL MW BUILDUP]
```

---

## 4.7 Block Diagram — Urea-Formaldehyde Curing Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[UREA AND FORMALDEHYDE] --> stepA[STAGE A METHYLOLATION]
    stepA --> stepB[STAGE B CONDENSATION]
    stepB --> stepC[STAGE C CROSS LINKING WITH HEAT]
    stepC --> node2[INFUSIBLE INSOLUBLE UF NETWORK]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between Novolac and Resol resins.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Novolac | Resol |
|---|---|---|
| Formaldehyde : Phenol | Less than 1 (≈ 0.85) | Greater than 1 (≈ 1.5–2.5) |
| Catalyst | Acidic (Oxalic acid / H₂SO₄) | Basic (NaOH / NH₄OH) |
| Structure | Linear | Branched |
| Functional groups in resin | Free –OH | Free –CH₂OH + –OH |
| Hardening | Requires hexamethylenetetramine | Self-curing on heating |
| Application | Moulding powder, Bakelite | Adhesives, laminates |

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is Carothers' equation? State its significance.**

**Model Answer:**
Carothers' equation relates the number-average degree of polymerization (DPₙ) to the extent of reaction (p):

$$DP_n = \frac{1}{(1-p)}$$

**Significance:**
- Shows that DP grows non-linearly as p → 1.
- At p = 0.99, DPₙ = 100; at p = 0.999, DPₙ = 1000.
- For high molecular weight, **conversion must exceed 99%** — explains why step-growth polymerizations need careful monomer balance and removal of by-product (Le Chatelier's principle).

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**a)** Describe the laboratory preparation of **Phenol-Formaldehyde resin (Novolac type)**. Include the reaction, mechanism, and curing process. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Write notes on the **Glyptal resin synthesis**, giving the reaction and any two industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Novolac Preparation**

**Reaction:**
Phenol + Formaldehyde (1 : 0.85) in presence of oxalic acid catalyst at reflux (100 °C) for 2 h.

**Mechanism (3 steps):**

1. **Hydroxymethylation:** Phenol (electron-rich at o, p) + HCHO → o/p-hydroxymethyl phenol.
2. **Condensation:** Two hydroxymethylphenols → methylene-bridged dimer with release of water.
3. **Chain Growth:** Repeated condensation → linear Novolac polymer.

**Reaction:**
$$n\, C_6H_5OH + (n-1)\, HCHO \xrightarrow{H^+} HO-[C_6H_3(OH)-CH_2]_n-H + (n-1)\, H_2O$$

**Curing:** Mixed with hexamethylenetetramine (Hexa, ~10%) and heated at 150–200 °C under pressure (~10 MPa) in mould. Hexa decomposes to formaldehyde and ammonia, providing additional cross-links.

**Final product:** Rigid, black/brown Bakelite — used for switchboards, combs, telephone housings.

**(b) Glyptal Resin**

**Reactants:** Phthalic anhydride + Glycerol (1 : 1 molar ratio approximately).

**Reaction (condensation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Phthalic anhydride} + \text{Glycerol} \xrightarrow{200{-}250\,^{\circ}C} \\
\text{Polyester with cross-links via secondary -OH of glycerol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Mechanism:** Phthalic anhydride ring opens with primary –OH of glycerol. The secondary –OH at C-2 enables **3D cross-linking** (key to its thermoset behavior).

**Industrial applications (any two):**
1. Surface coatings and lacquers for automobiles and appliances.
2. Binder for metal primers and decorative finishes.
3. Component of alkyd paints (modified with drying oils like linseed oil).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**a)** Outline the **mechanism of free-radical polymerization of styrene** using benzoyl peroxide as initiator. Show initiation, propagation, and termination steps. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Describe the **Viscose process for preparing regenerated cellulose**, listing the key chemical reactions involved. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Polystyrene Mechanism**

**Initiation:**
$$(C_6H_5COO)_2 \xrightarrow{80{-}90\,^{\circ}C} 2\, C_6H_5COO^{\bullet} \longrightarrow 2\, C_6H_5^{\bullet} + 2\, CO_2$$
$$C_6H_5^{\bullet} + CH_2=CH-C_6H_5 \longrightarrow C_6H_5-CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}H-C_6H_5 \quad \text{(benzylic radical)}$$

**Propagation (resonance-stabilized):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R-[CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}H(C_6H_5)]_n + CH_2=CH-C_6H_5 \longrightarrow \\
R-[CH_2-CH(C_6H_5)]_n-CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}H(C_6H_5)
\end{aligned}$$

The benzylic radical is stabilized by delocalization into the aromatic ring (3 resonance structures).

**Termination (coupling):**
$$P^{\bullet} + P^{\bullet} \longrightarrow P-P$$

**Final structure:** $[-CH_2-CH(C_6H_5)-]_n$

**(b) Viscose Process (Regenerated Cellulose)**

| Stage | Reagent / Conditions | Reaction |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Mercerization | 18% NaOH, 20 °C | Cellulose → Alkali cellulose (swelling) |
| 2. Pressing & Shredding | Mechanical | Removes excess alkali, increases surface area |
| 3. Aging | 25–30 °C, 24–48 h | Reduces DP to ~ 350 |
| 4. Xanthation | CS₂, 25–30 °C | Alkali cellulose → Sodium cellulose xanthate (orange viscose) |
| 5. Dissolution | Dilute NaOH | Forms 7–8% viscose dope |
| 6. Spinning | H₂SO₄ + Na₂SO₄ + ZnSO₄ bath | Xanthate decomposed → regenerated cellulose fibre/film |

**Key regeneration reaction:**
$$\text{Cell-O-CS-S-Na} + H_2SO_4 \longrightarrow \text{Cell-OH} + CS_2 + NaHSO_4$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Polymer Lab Viva/Written Exam:**
> 1. **Confusing Novolac and Resol:** Forgetting that Novolac is acid-catalyzed and Resol is base-catalyzed — costs **2 marks** minimum.
> 2. **Skipping initiator decomposition step:** Examiners want the **first equation** (e.g., BPO → radicals). Many students directly write monomer attack and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the curing step** in Novolac (Hexamine addition). Novolac alone is not Bakelite — it must be cross-linked with hexa.
> 4. **Writing "condensation polymerization" for Polyurethane** — it is technically a step-growth *addition* (no by-product). Examiners mark this strictly.
> 5. **Not balancing equations:** Ensure water molecules are correctly released in all condensation reactions.
> 6. **Mixing up Glyptal and Alkyd resin terminology** — Glyptal is the unmodified glycerol-phthalic anhydride resin; alkyds are modified with drying oils.
> 7. **Skipping resonance in polystyrene radical stabilization** — a key 1-mark point that examiners often probe in viva.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **PMMA**: Free-radical polymerization of MMA; BPO/AIBN initiator; 80 °C; product is transparent, hard (Tg ≈ 105 °C).
- **Polyacrylamide**: Aqueous FRP of acrylamide; APS + TEMED initiator; water-soluble white solid.
- **Polystyrene**: FRP of styrene; BPO at 80–90 °C; benzylic radical stabilized by resonance; Tg ≈ 100 °C.
- **Novolac**: Phenol : HCHO = 1 : < 1, **acid catalyst**, **linear**, **needs hexamine** to cure.
- **Resol**: Phenol : HCHO = 1 : > 1, **base catalyst**, **branched**, **self-curing** on heating.
- **Glyptal**: Phthalic anhydride + glycerol; secondary –OH enables cross-linking; used in paints/coatings.
- **Urea-Formaldehyde**: Urea : HCHO = 1 : 1.5; used as wood adhesive (particle board, plywood).
- **Melamine-Formaldehyde**: Melamine has 3 –NH₂ groups → 3D cross-linked, heat-resistant; used in dinnerware and Formica laminates.
- **Polyurethane**: Diisocyanate (TDI/MDI) + polyol; **no by-product**; flexible foams → rigid foams depending on polyol type.
- **Regenerated Cellulose**: 6-step Viscose process (Mercerization → Xanthation → Spinning); CS₂ is the key reagent; product is rayon/cellophane.
- **Carothers' equation**: $\overline{DP_n = \frac{1}{1-p}}$ — drives home the need for > 99% conversion in step-growth polymerizations.
- **Two major polymerization types** — Addition (chain-growth, vinyl monomers) and Condensation (step-growth, with small molecule release).
- **Industrial importance**: Phenolics → electrical parts; UF → particle board; MF → kitchenware; PU → foams and elastomers; PMMA → transparent sheets; PS → packaging; Polyacrylamide → water treatment.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Tip for KTU Exam:** When asked "write the synthesis of X," always follow this 4-point structure: **(i) Reaction equation (balanced) → (ii) Reagents and conditions → (iii) Step-wise mechanism (3 steps) → (iv) Industrial applications**. This structure fetches full marks consistently in the 14-mark questions.

topics.title: Quantitative Estimation of Monomers: Aniline, Phenol, Acetone, Ethyl Acetate, Formaldehyde, Acrylonitrile, Urea, Glycol, and Methyl methacrylate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL407 - POLYMER PREPARATION AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Quantitative Estimation of Monomers: Aniline, Phenol, Acetone, Ethyl Acetate, Formaldehyde, Acrylonitrile, Urea, Glycol, and Methyl methacrylate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quantitative Estimation of Monomers** refers to the set of standardized **wet chemical (volumetric/analytical) procedures** used in polymer chemistry laboratories to determine the exact mass, volume, or percentage purity of a given monomer sample. Since monomers are the reactive building blocks of polymers, their precise quantification is critical for calculating stoichiometric feed ratios, predicting molecular weight, and ensuring reproducible polymer synthesis.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCPOL407), this experiment covers nine industrially significant monomers, each analyzed through a specific reaction pathway that exploits a unique functional group:

- **Aniline (Ar-NH₂)** – Bromination of the aromatic ring.
- **Phenol (Ar-OH)** – Bromination of the aromatic ring.
- **Acetone (ketone, C=O)** – Oximation with hydroxylamine hydrochloride.
- **Ethyl Acetate (ester, -COO-)** – Alkaline saponification (back titration).
- **Formaldehyde (HCHO, aldehyde)** – Bisulfite addition / Iodometric oxidation.
- **Acrylonitrile (CH₂=CHCN, vinyl)** – Electrophilic addition of bromine across the C=C bond.
- **Urea (NH₂-CO-NH₂)** – Kjeldahl nitrogen digestion-distillation.
- **Glycol (HOCH₂CH₂OH, 1,2-diol)** – Periodic acid (HIO₄) oxidative cleavage.
- **Methyl Methacrylate (CH₂=C(CH₃)COOCH₃, vinyl)** – Bromine addition to the C=C bond.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Every estimation rests on a single fundamental equation: *Equivalents of analyte = Equivalents of titrant*. Students are expected to derive the molar mass ratio for each method, as the marks are awarded for **correct stoichiometric balancing** of the analyte-titrant reaction, not just the final answer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **quantitative estimation** like weighing sugar in a grocery store with an old-fashioned balance scale. Instead of placing the sugar directly on the scale, you dissolve it in water and react it with a known "weight" of a standard chemical (the titrant). By measuring *how much titrant is consumed*, you back-calculate *how much sugar (monomer) was actually present*. The reaction is the "ruler," and the titrant is the "calibrated weight."

**Real-world analogy:**
- **Bromination (Aniline, Phenol, MMA, Acrylonitrile):** Like counting the holes punched in a paper sheet — each monomer consumes a fixed number of "Br₂ punches," so the consumed Br₂ directly tells you the monomer count.
- **Saponification (Ethyl Acetate):** Like a chemical seesaw — a known excess of NaOH is added, and the leftover is measured.
- **Kjeldahl (Urea):** Like a nitrogen-stripping machine — the nitrogen in urea is converted to ammonia and "captured" in acid, then weighed indirectly.
- **Periodate (Glycol):** Like a precise molecular scissor — HIO₄ cuts only the C–C bond between two adjacent -OH groups, and the products are then measured.

### 1.3 Key Standard Constants & Units

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Molar mass of aniline (C₆H₅NH₂) | **93.13** | g·mol⁻¹ |
| Molar mass of phenol (C₆H₅OH) | **94.11** | g·mol⁻¹ |
| Molar mass of acetone ((CH₃)₂CO) | **58.08** | g·mol⁻¹ |
| Molar mass of ethyl acetate (CH₃COOC₂H₅) | **88.11** | g·mol⁻¹ |
| Molar mass of formaldehyde (HCHO) | **30.03** | g·mol⁻¹ |
| Molar mass of acrylonitrile (CH₂=CHCN) | **53.06** | g·mol⁻¹ |
| Molar mass of urea (NH₂CONH₂) | **60.06** | g·mol⁻¹ |
| Molar mass of ethylene glycol (HOCH₂CH₂OH) | **62.07** | g·mol⁻¹ |
| Molar mass of methyl methacrylate (MMA) | **100.12** | g·mol⁻¹ |
| Atomic mass of nitrogen (N) | **14.01** | g·mol⁻¹ |
| Equivalents of Br₂ consumed per mole of aniline/phenol | **3** | eq·mol⁻¹ |
| Equivalents of Br₂ per mole of acrylonitrile/MMA | **1** | eq·mol⁻¹ |
| Equivalents of NaOH per mole of acetone | **1** | eq·mol⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must be able to write balanced chemical equations for the analyte-reagent step, derive the **molarity-to-mass conversion factor**, and report the result in **g/L (for solutions)** or **% purity (for solids)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Estimation Methods

The nine monomers are estimated by **four distinct reaction principles**, each exploiting a characteristic functional group.

**A. Bromination (Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution / Electrophilic Addition)**

- **Monomers covered:** Aniline, Phenol, Acrylonitrile, Methyl methacrylate.
- **Principle:** Br₂ is generated in situ from **KBrO₃ + KBr in acidic medium**. The Br₂ reacts with the monomer; the **excess Br₂** liberates I₂ from KI, which is then back-titrated with standard **Na₂S₂O₃ (sodium thiosulfate)** using starch indicator.
- **Why Br₂ is preferred:** It is a strong yet controllable electrophile, reacts quantitatively, and its excess can be measured iodometrically with very high precision (detection limit ~0.1 mg).

**B. Oximation (Carbonyl Reagent Method)**

- **Monomer covered:** Acetone.
- **Principle:** Hydroxylamine hydrochloride (NH₂OH·HCl) reacts with the carbonyl group to form an **oxime** and releases an equivalent of **HCl**. The liberated HCl is titrated with standard NaOH using phenolphthalein/methyl orange.

**C. Saponification (Alkaline Hydrolysis, Back-Titration)**

- **Monomer covered:** Ethyl acetate.
- **Principle:** A known excess of standard **NaOH** is added to hydrolyze the ester. The unreacted NaOH is back-titrated with standard **HCl** using phenolphthalein.

**D. Carbonyl/Schiff-Base & Iodometric Methods**

- **Monomer covered:** Formaldehyde.
- **Principle (Iodometric):** HCHO is oxidized to formic acid by a known excess of **I₂ in alkaline medium**. The unreacted I₂ is back-titrated with Na₂S₂O₃.

**E. Nitrogen Estimation (Kjeldahl Digestion)**

- **Monomer covered:** Urea.
- **Principle:** Concentrated **H₂SO₄ + CuSO₄ catalyst** digests organic N to **NH₄HSO₄**. NaOH is added to release NH₃, which is distilled into a known volume of standard **H₂SO₄**. The unreacted H₂SO₄ is back-titrated with NaOH.

**F. Periodate Oxidation (Malaprade Reaction)**

- **Monomer covered:** Ethylene glycol.
- **Principle:** **HIO₄ (periodic acid)** cleaves the vicinal diol C–C bond to give **2 HCHO + HIO₃ + H₂O**. The iodic acid (HIO₃) is then measured iodometrically.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Monomer | Core Reaction | Stoichiometry (mol analyte : mol titrant) | Calculation Formula |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Aniline** | C₆H₅NH₂ + 3Br₂ → C₆H₂Br₃NH₂ + 3HBr | 1 : 3 (Br₂) ; 1 : 6 (Na₂S₂O₃) | $\text{Mass of aniline} = \dfrac{93.13}{6} \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{thio}$ |
| 2 | **Phenol** | C₆H₅OH + 3Br₂ → C₆H₂Br₃OH + 3HBr | 1 : 3 (Br₂) ; 1 : 6 (Na₂S₂O₃) | $\text{Mass of phenol} = \dfrac{94.11}{6} \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{thio}$ |
| 3 | **Acetone** | (CH₃)₂CO + NH₂OH·HCl → (CH₃)₂C=NOH + HCl | 1 : 1 (HCl) ; 1 : 1 (NaOH) | $\text{Mass of acetone} = \dfrac{58.08}{1000} \times V_{NaOH} \times M_{NaOH}$ |
| 4 | **Ethyl Acetate** | CH₃COOC₂H₅ + NaOH → CH₃COONa + C₂H₅OH | 1 : 1 (NaOH) | $\text{Mass of EtOAc} = \dfrac{88.11}{1000} \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{HCl}$ |
| 5 | **Formaldehyde** | HCHO + I₂ + 3NaOH → HCOONa + 2NaI + 2H₂O | 1 : 1 (I₂) ; 1 : 2 (Na₂S₂O₃) | $\text{Mass of HCHO} = \dfrac{30.03}{2000} \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{thio}$ |
| 6 | **Acrylonitrile** | CH₂=CHCN + Br₂ → CH₂BrCHBrCN | 1 : 1 (Br₂) ; 1 : 2 (Na₂S₂O₃) | $\text{Mass of AN} = \dfrac{53.06}{2000} \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{thio}$ |
| 7 | **Urea** | NH₂CONH₂ + H₂SO₄ → (NH₄)₂SO₄ ; then NH₃ + H₂SO₄ (excess) | 1 mol urea : 1 mol N : 1 mol NH₃ : 1 mol H₂SO₄ | $\%\text{ N} = \dfrac{1.401 \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{HCl}}{W}$ |
| 8 | **Glycol** | HOCH₂CH₂OH + HIO₄ → 2HCHO + HIO₃ + H₂O | 1 : 1 (HIO₄ → HIO₃) ; 1 : 4 (Na₂S₂O₃) | $\text{Mass of glycol} = \dfrac{62.07}{4000} \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{thio}$ |
| 9 | **MMA** | CH₂=C(CH₃)COOCH₃ + Br₂ → CH₂BrCBr(CH₃)COOCH₃ | 1 : 1 (Br₂) ; 1 : 2 (Na₂S₂O₃) | $\text{Mass of MMA} = \dfrac{100.12}{2000} \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{thio}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Used:** $V_b$ = blank titre value, $V_s$ = sample titre value, $M_{thio}$ = molarity of thiosulfate, $M_{HCl}$ = molarity of HCl, $M_{NaOH}$ = molarity of NaOH, $W$ = weight of sample (in grams), 1.401 = $14.01 \times \dfrac{100}{1000}$ (factor for %N).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aniline & Phenol estimation** is essential in the **polyurethane / phenolic resin industry** for quality control of feedstocks. Off-spec aniline leads to inconsistent MDI/PMDI production.
- **Acetone & Ethyl Acetate estimation** is the backbone of **solvent purity certification** in pharmaceutical and polymer coating industries (e.g., PMMA casting, nail lacquers).
- **Formaldehyde estimation** is a regulatory requirement in **textile, plywood, and urea-formaldehyde resin** industries due to its carcinogenic nature (IARC Group 1).
- **Acrylonitrile & MMA estimation** is critical for **ABS, PAN fiber, and acrylic sheet** manufacturing, where even 0.1% impurity drastically alters molecular weight and impact strength.
- **Urea estimation** in fertilizer-grade urea and UF resins ensures **nitrogen content compliance** with FCO (Fertilizer Control Order).
- **Glycol estimation** controls the **PET / polyester fiber** industry — MEG (monoethylene glycol) purity directly affects polymer chain length.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bromination Method (Aniline as Reference Case)

The bromination reaction sequence contains **three coupled sub-reactions**. Each must be balanced for the stoichiometric factor to be derived correctly.

**Step 1 — Generation of Br₂ in situ:**

$$\text{KBrO}_3 + 5\text{KBr} + 6\text{HCl} \rightarrow 3\text{Br}_2 + 6\text{KCl} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Bromination of aniline (electrophilic aromatic substitution at 2, 4, 6 positions):**

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NH}_2 + 3\text{Br}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_2\text{Br}_3\text{NH}_2 + 3\text{HBr}$$

**Step 3 — Reaction of excess Br₂ with KI:**

$$\text{Br}_2 + 2\text{KI} \rightarrow 2\text{KBr} + \text{I}_2$$

**Step 4 — Iodometric titration of liberated I₂ with sodium thiosulfate:**

$$\text{I}_2 + 2\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6$$

**Stoichiometric Chain Derivation (Equivalents approach):**

By inspection of the four balanced equations, the mole relations are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ mol aniline} &\equiv 3 \text{ mol Br}_2 \\
1 \text{ mol Br}_2 &\equiv 1 \text{ mol I}_2 \\
1 \text{ mol I}_2 &\equiv 2 \text{ mol Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, by transitivity of equivalence:

$$1 \text{ mol aniline} \equiv 3 \text{ mol I}_2 \equiv 6 \text{ mol Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3$$

The **normality (N)** relationship for the analyte vs. the titrant becomes:

$$N_{aniline} \times V_{aniline} = 6 \times N_{thio} \times V_{thio}$$

where $V_{thio} = V_b - V_s$ (the *difference* between blank and sample titre values is the volume consumed by *excess* Br₂; the analyte's Br₂ consumption is indirectly measured by the *unreacted* Br₂).

**Final mass formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of aniline (g)} &= \dfrac{\text{Equivalent weight of aniline}}{1000} \times V_{net} \times M_{thio} \\
&= \dfrac{93.13 / 6}{1000} \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{thio} \\
&= 0.015522 \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{thio}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Bridge:** The factor **6** in the denominator (the "6-thiosulfate factor") arises because each aniline molecule consumes 3 Br₂, each Br₂ liberates 1 I₂, and each I₂ requires 2 thiosulfate. The chain is **3 × 1 × 2 = 6**. This same logic applies to phenol (factor 6), acrylonitrile (factor 2), and MMA (factor 2).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Oximation Method (Acetone)

**Core reaction:**

$$(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C=O} + \text{NH}_2\text{OH}\cdot\text{HCl} \rightarrow (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C=NOH} + \text{HCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> Note: The HCl is *liberated*, not consumed, in this step.

**Equivalence:**

$$1 \text{ mol acetone} \equiv 1 \text{ mol HCl} \equiv 1 \text{ mol NaOH}$$

**Final mass formula:**

$$\text{Mass of acetone (g)} = \dfrac{58.08}{1000} \times V_{NaOH} \times M_{NaOH}$$

**Worked numerical example:**

Suppose 25.0 mL of an acetone solution is treated with excess hydroxylamine hydrochloride and titrated with 0.1 N NaOH, requiring 18.5 mL.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of acetone} &= \dfrac{58.08}{1000} \times 18.5 \times 0.1 \\
&= 0.05808 \times 1.85 \\
&= 0.1074 \text{ g} \\
\text{Concentration in g/L} &= \dfrac{0.1074 \times 1000}{25.0} = 4.298 \text{ g/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Saponification Method (Ethyl Acetate)

**Step 1 — Hydrolysis (saponification):**

$$\text{CH}_3\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{NaOH (excess)} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COONa} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$

**Step 2 — Back-titration of unreacted NaOH with standard HCl:**

$$\text{NaOH (unreacted)} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Equivalence:**

$$1 \text{ mol ethyl acetate} \equiv 1 \text{ mol NaOH (consumed)}$$

**Final mass formula:**

$$\text{Mass of EtOAc (g)} = \dfrac{88.11}{1000} \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{HCl}$$

where $V_b$ = titre of HCl for blank (no ester, full NaOH reacts), $V_s$ = titre of HCl for sample (less NaOH remains, so less HCl is needed ⇒ $V_s < V_b$).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Iodometric Method (Formaldehyde)

**Step 1 — Oxidation of HCHO in alkaline medium:**

$$\text{HCHO} + \text{I}_2 + 3\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{HCOONa} + 2\text{NaI} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> The formate is *not* oxidized further under these conditions; I₂ is consumed stoichiometrically.

**Step 2 — Acidification liberates I₂ from the remaining iodate/iodide, then titration with thiosulfate:**

$$2\text{NaI (residual)} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{HI} + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$$
$$\text{HCHO-unreacted I}_2 + 2\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6$$

**Equivalence:**

$$1 \text{ mol HCHO} \equiv 1 \text{ mol I}_2 \equiv 2 \text{ mol Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3$$

**Final mass formula:**

$$\text{Mass of HCHO (g)} = \dfrac{30.03}{2000} \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{thio}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Kjeldahl Method (Urea)

**Step 1 — Digestion (converts organic N to ammonium sulfate):**

$$\text{NH}_2\text{CONH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 (\text{conc.}) \xrightarrow{\text{CuSO}_4, \Delta} (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

**Step 2 — Distillation of ammonia into known excess of standard acid:**

$$(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{NH}_3 \uparrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$2\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 (\text{excess}) \rightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4$$

**Step 3 — Back-titration of unreacted H₂SO₄ with standard NaOH:**

$$\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 (\text{unreacted}) + 2\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Equivalence for %N calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ mol urea} &\equiv 2 \text{ mol N} \equiv 2 \text{ mol NH}_3 \equiv 1 \text{ mol H}_2\text{SO}_4 \\
1 \text{ mol H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ reacted} &\equiv 2 \text{ mol NaOH} \text{ consumed} \\
\text{Volume of HCl equivalent to NH}_3 &= (V_b - V_s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final %N formula (KTU standard form):**

$$\%\text{N} = \dfrac{1.401 \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{HCl}}{W}$$

where $W$ = weight of urea sample in grams. (The factor 1.401 = $14.01 \times \dfrac{100}{1000}$ embeds the atomic mass of N and unit conversion.)

**% Purity of urea:**

$$\%\text{ Urea} = \%\text{N} \times \dfrac{60.06}{2 \times 14.01} = \%\text{N} \times 2.143$$

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6: Periodate Method (Ethylene Glycol)

**Step 1 — Malaprade cleavage of the vicinal diol:**

$$\text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{HIO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{HCHO} + \text{HIO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Iodometric measurement of HIO₃ (which is reduced back to I⁻ by KI, liberating I₂):**

$$\text{HIO}_3 + 5\text{KI} + 6\text{HCl} \rightarrow 3\text{I}_2 + 5\text{KCl} + \text{KIO} \cdot \text{etc.}$$

Simplified: $1 \text{ mol HIO}_3 \equiv 3 \text{ mol I}_2 \equiv 6 \text{ mol Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3$

**Step 3 — Titration of liberated I₂:**

$$\text{I}_2 + 2\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6$$

**Equivalence (using 1 : 6 stoichiometry):**

$$1 \text{ mol glycol} \equiv 1 \text{ mol HIO}_4 \equiv 1 \text{ mol HIO}_3 \equiv 6 \text{ mol Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3$$

**Final mass formula:**

$$\text{Mass of glycol (g)} = \dfrac{62.07}{6000} \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{thio}$$

### 3.7 Analytical Master Matrix

| Monomer | Method | Molar Mass (g/mol) | Equiv. Factor to Na₂S₂O₃ | Final Formula Coefficient |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aniline | Bromination | 93.13 | 6 | $\dfrac{93.13}{6000} = 0.01552$ |
| Phenol | Bromination | 94.11 | 6 | $\dfrac{94.11}{6000} = 0.01569$ |
| Acetone | Oximation | 58.08 | (NaOH : 1) | $\dfrac{58.08}{1000} = 0.05808$ |
| Ethyl Acetate | Saponification | 88.11 | (HCl : 1) | $\dfrac{88.11}{1000} = 0.08811$ |
| Formaldehyde | Iodometric | 30.03 | 2 | $\dfrac{30.03}{2000} = 0.01502$ |
| Acrylonitrile | Bromination (C=C) | 53.06 | 2 | $\dfrac{53.06}{2000} = 0.02653$ |
| Urea | Kjeldahl | 60.06 | (HCl : 1) | 1.401 factor for %N |
| Glycol | Periodate | 62.07 | 6 | $\dfrac{62.07}{6000} = 0.01035$ |
| MMA | Bromination (C=C) | 100.12 | 2 | $\dfrac{100.12}{2000} = 0.05006$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Analytical Workflow (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of the entire estimation protocol**, mapping the decision logic from sample weighing through final calculation. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** rather than a physical drawing, in accordance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sampleA[Weigh or Pipette Monomer Sample] --> idA{Functional Group?}
    idA -->|Ar-NH2 or Ar-OH| routeA[Bromination Method]
    idA -->|C=O Ketone| routeB[Oximation Method]
    idA -->|Ester| routeC[Saponification Method]
    idA -->|Aldehyde| routeD[Iodometric Method]
    idA -->|C=C Vinyl| routeE[Addition Bromination]
    idA -->|Organic N| routeF[Kjeldahl Digestion]
    idA -->|Vicinal Diol| routeG[Periodate Method]

    routeA --> reactA[React with KBrO3 KBr in HCl]
    routeE --> reactE[React with Standard Br2 Solution]
    reactA --> addKIa[Add excess KI to liberate I2]
    reactE --> addKIb[Add excess KI to liberate I2]
    addKIa --> titA[Titrate I2 with Na2S2O3 using starch indicator]
    addKIb --> titA

    routeB --> reactB[React with NH2OH HCl]
    reactB --> titB[Titrate liberated HCl with NaOH using phenolphthalein]

    routeC --> reactC[Add known excess NaOH]
    reactC --> titC[Back-titrate unreacted NaOH with HCl]

    routeD --> reactD[React HCHO with I2 in NaOH medium]
    reactD --> addKIc[Acidify with HCl, add KI]
    addKIc --> titD[Titrate I2 with Na2S2O3]

    routeF --> digestF[Digest with H2SO4 and CuSO4 catalyst]
    digestF --> distF[Distill NH3 into excess standard H2SO4]
    distF --> titF[Back-titrate unreacted H2SO4 with NaOH]

    routeG --> reactG[React glycol with HIO4]
    reactG --> addKId[Add KI and HCl]
    addKId --> titG[Titrate I2 with Na2S2O3]

    titA --> calcA[Apply bromination mass formula]
    titB --> calcB[Apply oximation mass formula]
    titC --> calcC[Apply saponification mass formula]
    titD --> calcD[Apply iodometric mass formula]
    titF --> calcF[Apply Kjeldahl percent N formula]
    titG --> calcG[Apply periodate mass formula]

    calcA --> reportA[Report g L or percent purity]
    calcB --> reportB[Report g L or percent purity]
    calcC --> reportC[Report g L or percent purity]
    calcD --> reportD[Report g L or percent purity]
    calcF --> reportF[Report percent N and percent urea]
    calcG --> reportG[Report g L or percent purity]
```

### 4.2 Bromination Sub-System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph generation["Step 1: In-Situ Br2 Generation"]
        kbro3[KBrO3 Standard] -->|with| kbr[KBr]
        kbr -->|acidified with| hcl1[HCl]
        hcl1 --> br2[Free Br2 Generated]
    end

    subgraph reaction["Step 2: Analyte Bromination"]
        br2 -->|reacts with| monomer[Monomer Sample]
        monomer --> product[Brominated Product + HBr]
    end

    subgraph quenching["Step 3: Quench Excess Br2"]
        br2excess[Excess Br2] -->|with| ki[KI Solution]
        ki --> i2[Equivalent I2 Liberated]
    end

    subgraph titration["Step 4: Iodometric Titration"]
        i2 -->|titrated with| thio[Na2S2O3]
        thio -->|indicator| starch[Starch Blue to Colorless]
    end

    generation --> reaction
    reaction --> quenching
    quenching --> titration

    titration --> vblank[Record Blank Titre Vb]
    titration --> vsample[Record Sample Titre Vs]
    vblank --> netvol[Vnet = Vb - Vs]
    vsample --> netvol
    netvol --> masscalc[Apply Mass Formula]
```

### 4.3 Kjeldahl Nitrogen Estimation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    urea[Urea Sample W grams] --> digest[Digest with Conc H2SO4 and CuSO4 catalyst at 400 degree C]
    digest --> nhsulph[NH4HSO4 in digestion flask]
    nhsulph --> addnaoh[Add excess NaOH]
    addnaoh --> nh3[NH3 Liberated]
    nh3 --> distil[Distil NH3 into known volume Vb of standard HCl]
    distil --> nh4cl[NH4Cl trapped in receiver]
    nh4cl --> backtitrate[Back-titrate unreacted HCl with standard NaOH]
    backtitrate --> vs[Record sample titre Vs]
    urea --> blankrun[Run blank without urea]
    blankrun --> vb[Record blank titre Vb]
    vb --> netv[Vnet = Vb - Vs]
    vs --> netv
    netv --> formula[Apply 1.401 times Vnet times M HCl divided by W]
    formula --> percentN[Percent N]
    percentN --> purity[Percent Urea = Percent N times 2.143]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
What is the principle involved in the estimation of aniline by the bromination method? Write the balanced chemical equation for the reaction between aniline and bromine.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Aniline contains an activated aromatic ring due to the strongly electron-donating -NH₂ group. It undergoes **electrophilic aromatic substitution** with bromine at the 2, 4, and 6 positions, consuming 3 moles of Br₂ per mole of aniline and forming **2,4,6-tribromoaniline**. The excess (unreacted) Br₂ is determined iodometrically by adding KI (which liberates I₂) and back-titrating with standard sodium thiosulfate using starch indicator.

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NH}_2 + 3\text{Br}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_2\text{Br}_3\text{NH}_2 + 3\text{HBr}$$

**(1 mark — principle; 1 mark — equation; 1 mark — explanation of iodometric back-titration.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Why is the Kjeldahl method used for estimating urea? What is the role of CuSO₄ in the digestion step?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Kjeldahl method is used to estimate **organic nitrogen** content. Since urea (NH₂CONH₂) contains two nitrogen atoms, digesting it with concentrated H₂SO₄ converts the organic N to inorganic ammonium sulfate. The amount of NH₃ liberated upon alkalization is then trapped in a known excess of standard acid and back-titrated.

**Role of CuSO₄:** CuSO₄ acts as a **catalyst** in the digestion step. It raises the boiling point of the digestion mixture, accelerates the oxidation of organic matter, and ensures complete conversion of organic N to (NH₄)₂SO₄ within a reasonable time. Without the catalyst, the digestion is incomplete and slow.

**(1 mark — reason for Kjeldahl; 1 mark — digestion equation; 1 mark — CuSO₄ role.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Model)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — KTU ESE Model Paper Style

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module Experiment 2]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the procedure for the **quantitative estimation of acetone** by the oximation method. Write the balanced chemical equation and derive the formula for calculating the mass of acetone. (7 Marks)

**(b)** 25.0 mL of an aqueous acetone solution is treated with excess hydroxylamine hydrochloride. The liberated HCl requires 22.4 mL of 0.1 N NaOH for neutralization. Calculate the concentration of acetone in g/L. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure (7 Marks):**

1. **Apparatus:** Burette, pipette, conical flask, indicator (phenolphthalein or methyl orange).
2. **Reagent:** Standard 0.1 N NaOH, hydroxylamine hydrochloride solution (10% in water, neutralized to phenolphthalein endpoint), distilled water.
3. **Procedure:**
   - Pipette 25.0 mL of the acetone solution into a clean conical flask.
   - Add 20 mL of neutralized hydroxylamine hydrochloride reagent.
   - Stopper the flask and allow it to stand for 10–15 minutes (or warm gently on a water bath at 50 °C for 5 min) to ensure complete oximation.
   - The carbonyl group of acetone reacts with NH₂OH·HCl to form the oxime, releasing an equivalent of HCl.
   - Titrate the liberated HCl against standard 0.1 N NaOH using phenolphthalein indicator until a faint pink endpoint is reached.
   - Note the titre volume $V_{NaOH}$.
4. **Balanced equation (1 mark):**

$$(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CO} + \text{NH}_2\text{OH}\cdot\text{HCl} \rightarrow (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C=NOH} + \text{HCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

5. **Derivation of mass formula (1 mark):**

Since 1 mol acetone liberates 1 mol HCl, which in turn reacts with 1 mol NaOH:

$$\text{Equivalents of acetone} = \text{Equivalents of NaOH}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{W_{acetone}}{58.08 / 1000} &= V_{NaOH} \times M_{NaOH} \\
W_{acetone} &= \dfrac{58.08}{1000} \times V_{NaOH} \times M_{NaOH}
\end{aligned}
$$

Concentration in g/L: $\text{Conc.} = \dfrac{W_{acetone} \times 1000}{V_{sample}}$

**(b) Numerical (7 Marks):**

Given: $V_{sample} = 25.0$ mL, $V_{NaOH} = 22.4$ mL, $M_{NaOH} = 0.1$ N.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of acetone in 25.0 mL} &= \dfrac{58.08}{1000} \times 22.4 \times 0.1 \\
&= 0.05808 \times 2.24 \\
&= 0.1301 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Concentration in g/L} &= \dfrac{0.1301 \times 1000}{25.0} \\
&= \boxed{5.204 \text{ g/L}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(Distribution: 1 mark — formula, 2 marks — substitution, 2 marks — final mass, 2 marks — final concentration.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module Experiment 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Kjeldahl method** for the estimation of urea. Write all the reactions involved and derive the formula for percentage of nitrogen. (7 Marks)

**(b)** 0.5 g of a urea sample was digested and the liberated ammonia was absorbed in 50.0 mL of 0.1 N H₂SO₄. The unreacted acid required 25.5 mL of 0.1 N NaOH for back-titration. Calculate the percentage purity of the urea sample. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Kjeldahl Method (7 Marks):**

1. **Principle:** Organic nitrogen is converted to ammonium sulfate by digestion with concentrated H₂SO₄, then liberated as NH₃ by adding excess NaOH, and finally absorbed in a known excess of standard acid. The unreacted acid is back-titrated.

2. **Reactions (3 marks for all three equations):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NH}_2\text{CONH}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 &\xrightarrow{\text{CuSO}_4, \Delta} (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow + \text{other products} \\
(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{NaOH} &\rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{NH}_3 \uparrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
2\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 (\text{excess}) &\rightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 \\
\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 (\text{unreacted}) + 2\text{NaOH} &\rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

3. **Derivation of %N formula (3 marks):**

Volume of H₂SO₄ neutralized by NH₃ = (Volume of H₂SO₄ for blank − Volume of H₂SO₄ for sample) × Normality

$$\text{Volume of standard HCl equivalent to NH}_3 = (V_b - V_s)$$

Equivalents of N = Equivalents of HCl reacted

$$\dfrac{W_N}{14.01 / 1000} = (V_b - V_s) \times M_{HCl}$$

$$\%\text{N} = \dfrac{14.01 \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{HCl} \times 100}{W_{sample} \times 1000} = \dfrac{1.401 \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{HCl}}{W_{sample}}$$

**(b) Numerical (7 Marks):**

Given: $W_{urea} = 0.5$ g, $V_{H_2SO_4} = 50.0$ mL of 0.1 N, $V_{NaOH, sample} = 25.5$ mL of 0.1 N.

In the back-titration, NaOH reacts with excess H₂SO₄. First, find the blank titre of NaOH that neutralizes 50.0 mL of 0.1 N H₂SO₄:

$$V_b = \dfrac{50.0 \times 0.1}{0.1} = 50.0 \text{ mL}$$

$$V_{net} = V_b - V_s = 50.0 - 25.5 = 24.5 \text{ mL}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{N} &= \dfrac{1.401 \times 24.5 \times 0.1}{0.5} \\
&= \dfrac{1.401 \times 2.45}{0.5} \\
&= \dfrac{3.4324}{0.5} \\
&= 6.865\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\%\text{ Urea} = \%\text{N} \times \dfrac{60.06}{28.02} = 6.865 \times 2.143 = 14.71\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative approach using direct factor:** In some KTU solutions, the formula is presented as $\%\text{ Urea} = \dfrac{1.401 \times (V_b - V_s) \times M_{HCl} \times 2.143}{W}$, which gives the same result. The factor 2.143 = 60.06/28.02 = 60.06/(2 × 14.01) is the **urea-to-nitrogen molar mass ratio**.

**(Distribution: 2 marks — blank calculation, 2 marks — %N, 3 marks — %urea.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Molar mass vs. equivalent mass confusion:** Students frequently write the molar mass directly in the numerator without dividing by the equivalence factor. For aniline (factor 6), this gives a 6× inflated answer.
> 2. **Blank–Sample sign error:** In back-titrations, $V_b > V_s$ (more titrant is needed for the blank since it has more acid/base to neutralize). Reversing this gives a **negative** titre and zero marks.
> 3. **Kjeldahl factor misuse:** The factor 1.401 already includes the conversion to %; do **not** multiply by 100 again.
> 4. **Bromination equation errors:** For aromatic bromination (aniline/phenol), the factor is 6, but for vinyl bromination (acrylonitrile/MMA), the factor is **2** (one C=C consumes one Br₂, giving one I₂, requiring two thiosulfate). Mixing these up is the single most common mistake.
> 5. **Periodate equivalence:** Students often assume 1 mol glycol = 2 mol HIO₄, but only **1 mol HIO₄** is consumed in the Malaprade reaction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Bromination methods** always follow the chain: **Monomer + Br₂ → Brominated product**; **excess Br₂ + 2KI → I₂**; **I₂ + 2Na₂S₂O₃ → products**. Starch indicator turns blue-black in presence of I₂ and colorless at endpoint.
- **Equivalence factor** = number of moles of Na₂S₂O₃ (or NaOH/HCl) reacting per mole of monomer. For aniline and phenol = **6**; for acrylonitrile, MMA, and formaldehyde = **2**; for ethylene glycol = **6**; for acetone and ethyl acetate = **1** (to NaOH/HCl).
- **Blank titration** ($V_b$) is essential for all back-titration methods (bromination, saponification, Kjeldahl, periodate). It determines the total amount of titrant available before analyte consumption.
- **Aniline** and **phenol** both give 2,4,6-tribromo derivatives; the difference is in the substituent (–NH₂ vs –OH).
- **Acetone estimation** uses **NH₂OH·HCl** and titrates the **liberated HCl** — this is **NOT a back-titration**; no blank is needed unless reagents are acidic.
- **Ethyl acetate estimation** uses **saponification with NaOH**, followed by back-titration with **HCl**. Reflux for 30 min is recommended to ensure complete hydrolysis.
- **Formaldehyde iodometric** method requires **alkaline medium** for oxidation; acidification (with HCl) is done *after* reaction completion to liberate residual I₂ for titration.
- **Urea estimation** uses **Kjeldahl digestion**; the **1.401 factor** is the universally applied normality constant for nitrogen (14.01 × 100/1000). %urea = %N × 2.143.
- **Glycol estimation** by **periodate** is highly specific for **vicinal diols** (1,2-diols). 1,3- or 1,4-diols do **not** react — this is the diagnostic advantage of the Malaprade reaction.
- **Methyl methacrylate (MMA)** contains both an ester and a C=C; only the C=C is reactive in the **bromination** method, so the factor remains 2 (not 1).
- **KBrO₃ + KBr** generates Br₂ *in situ* — this is preferred over direct Br₂ use because Br₂ is volatile and toxic, and KBrO₃ is a stable, primary-standard solid.
- **Hydroxylamine hydrochloride** reagent must be **neutralized to phenolphthalein endpoint** before use, because the commercial salt is acidic and would give a falsely high titre.
- **End-point colors:** Starch = colorless (from blue-black); Phenolphthalein = faint pink (from colorless); Methyl orange = orange (from red).
- **Indicator choice rule:** Iodometric titrations (Br₂ methods) → **starch**; Acid–base titrations involving weak acid/strong base → **phenolphthalein**; Strong acid/strong base with weak acid product → **methyl orange**.
- **All results** must be reported with **correct significant figures** (typically 3–4 SF), proper **units** (g/L or % purity), and a **replicate error** statement (e.g., "mean of three concordant readings ± 0.05 mL").
- **Safety notes:** Br₂ vapors are corrosive — use fume hood. HCHO is a known carcinogen — handle in fume hood with gloves. Concentrated H₂SO₄ is highly corrosive — always add acid to water, never the reverse.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Determination of Molecular Weight – The Polymer Context

The **molecular weight of a polymer** is a fundamental property that dictates its mechanical strength, rheological behaviour, processing characteristics, and end-use applications. Unlike small molecules, polymers do not possess a single, discrete molecular weight; instead, they exhibit a distribution of chain lengths, which is why we speak of *average molecular weights* ($M_n$, $M_w$, $M_v$, $M_z$). The **viscosity method** and the **end group analysis** are two classical, experiment-based techniques routinely performed in the Polymer Preparation and Analysis Lab to determine different averages of molecular weight.

### 1.1.1 Molecular Weight by Viscosity Method (Viscometry)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> The viscosity method is a *relative* method for determining the **viscosity-average molecular weight ($M_v$)** of a polymer by measuring the intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$ of a dilute polymer solution and relating it to molecular weight through the **Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation**:
> $$[\eta] = K M_v^{\alpha}$$
> where $K$ and $\alpha$ are empirical constants characteristic of a given polymer–solvent–temperature system.

**Conceptual Analogy – The "Honey vs Water" Intuition:**

Imagine pouring honey and water through identical funnels. The *thinner* water flows quickly (low viscosity), while honey resists flow (high viscosity). Now imagine dissolving tiny invisible "spaghetti strands" (polymer chains) in water. The longer the strands, the more they tangle with one another, and the more they resist flow. By carefully measuring *how much* the flow is slowed down, we can infer *how long* the chains are — and therefore, *how heavy* the molecules are. This is precisely what the viscosity method does. The longer the polymer chain, the larger its molecular weight, the higher the solution viscosity.

### 1.1.2 Molecular Weight by End Group Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> End group analysis is an *absolute* method for determining the **number-average molecular weight ($M_n$)** of linear polymers that contain chemically identifiable end groups (typically $-\text{COOH}$ and $-\text{OH}$ in polyesters, or $-\text{NH}_2$ and $-\text{COOH}$ in polyamides). The method is based on quantitatively estimating the number of end groups per unit mass of polymer.

**Conceptual Analogy – "Counting the Doors of a Building":**

Imagine a long street of identical attached houses (polymer chains). Each house has exactly two doors — one at each end (the two end groups). If you walk along the street and count every door, you can calculate how many houses there are. The number of houses tells you the *number* of polymer molecules. Knowing the total mass of all houses and the *number* of houses, you can compute the average weight of one house (the $M_n$). In end group analysis, we *count* the chemical "doors" (functional groups) at the chain termini.

### 1.1.3 Why Two Different Methods Exist — The $M_v$ vs $M_n$ Distinction

| Feature | Viscosity Method | End Group Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| Average obtained | $M_v$ (viscosity-average) | $M_n$ (number-average) |
| Absolute / Relative | Relative (needs calibration constants $K$, $\alpha$) | Absolute (stoichiometry-based) |
| Applicable to | All soluble polymers | Only polymers with detectable end groups |
| Typical polymers | Polystyrene, PMMA, PVC, PE derivatives | Polyesters, polyamides, polyurethanes |
| Instrumentation | Ostwald / Ubbelohde viscometer | Burette, pH meter, conductometer |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to *distinguish* between these two methods. Always mention (a) the type of average obtained, (b) the principle, and (c) the nature (absolute vs relative).

### 1.1.4 The Role of the Lab (PCPOL407)

In **PCPOL407 – Polymer Preparation and Analysis Lab**, this experiment is conducted in two halves:
1. **Half A – Viscosity Method:** Prepare dilute solutions of a known polymer (commonly polystyrene or polyvinyl alcohol) in an appropriate solvent, measure flow times using an Ostwald viscometer, and determine $M_v$ from the Mark–Houwink plot.
2. **Half B – End Group Analysis:** Titrate the acid end groups of a polyester (commonly prepared in Module Experiment 1, e.g., from adipic acid + ethylene glycol / hexamethylenediamine) against standard alkali and compute $M_n$.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background of the Viscosity Method

### 2.1.1 Definitions of Viscosity Terms

When a polymer is dissolved in a solvent, the viscosity of the solution increases over that of the pure solvent. The *extent* of this increase is measured through several defined viscosity terms, which the student *must* memorise for KTU exams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Let $t$ = flow time of polymer solution; $t_0$ = flow time of pure solvent through the same viscometer. Let $c$ = concentration of polymer (g/dL or g/100 mL).

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Relative viscosity | $\eta_{rel}$ | Ratio of solution viscosity to solvent viscosity | $\eta_{rel} = \dfrac{\eta}{\eta_0} = \dfrac{t}{t_0}$ |
| Specific viscosity | $\eta_{sp}$ | Fractional increase in viscosity caused by polymer | $\eta_{sp} = \eta_{rel} - 1 = \dfrac{t - t_0}{t_0}$ |
| Reduced viscosity | $\eta_{red}$ | Viscosity number per unit concentration | $\eta_{red} = \dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c}$ |
| Inherent viscosity | $\eta_{inh}$ | Logarithmic viscosity number per unit concentration | $\eta_{inh} = \dfrac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c}$ |
| Intrinsic viscosity | $[\eta]$ | Limiting value at infinite dilution | $[\eta] = \displaystyle \lim_{c \to 0} \dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = \displaystyle \lim_{c \to 0} \dfrac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c}$ |

**Why these definitions exist:** $\eta_{rel}$ and $\eta_{sp}$ depend on concentration, which makes them *non-fundamental*. By extrapolating to zero concentration, the polymer–polymer interactions vanish, leaving only polymer–solvent interactions — a property *intrinsic* to the polymer chain itself. This limiting value, $[\eta]$, has units of $\text{mL/g}$ (or $\text{dL/g}$).

### 2.1.2 The Two Extrapolation Equations

Two linear equations are used to obtain $[\eta]$ from experimental $\eta_{sp}/c$ and $\ln \eta_{rel}/c$ data:

**Huggins Equation:**
$$\dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 \, c$$

**Kraemer Equation:**
$$\dfrac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c} = [\eta] + k_K [\eta]^2 \, c$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Here, $k_H$ is the **Huggins constant** (typically 0.3 – 0.4 for good solvents) and $k_K$ is the **Kraemer constant**. Note that $k_H + k_K \approx 0.5$ for flexible polymers in good solvents. A plot of $\eta_{sp}/c$ versus $c$ and $\ln \eta_{rel}/c$ versus $c$ gives two straight lines that share a common intercept equal to $[\eta]$.

### 2.1.3 The Mark–Houwink–Sakurada Equation

Once $[\eta]$ is obtained, the **viscosity-average molecular weight** $M_v$ is calculated using:

$$[\eta] = K M_v^{\alpha}$$

$$\boxed{M_v = \left( \dfrac{[\eta]}{K} \right)^{1/\alpha}}$$

where $K$ and $\alpha$ are **Mark–Houwink constants** that depend on the polymer, solvent, and temperature.

### 2.1.4 Typical $K$ and $\alpha$ Values (Reference Table for KTU Lab)

| Polymer | Solvent | Temperature (°C) | $K \times 10^{3}$ (mL/g) | $\alpha$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Polystyrene | Toluene | 25 | 17 | 0.69 |
| Polystyrene | Benzene | 25 | 9.18 | 0.74 |
| Polystyrene | THF | 25 | 16 | 0.71 |
| PMMA | Acetone | 25 | 7.5 | 0.70 |
| PMMA | Benzene | 25 | 5.5 | 0.76 |
| PVC | Cyclohexanone | 25 | 13.7 | 0.78 |
| Poly(vinyl alcohol) | Water | 30 | 66.6 | 0.64 |
| Polyethylene | Decalin | 135 | 62 | 0.70 |
| Nylon-6,6 | m-Cresol | 25 | 320 | 0.62 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Always match the *exact* polymer–solvent–temperature trio before using $K$ and $\alpha$. Using PS–benzene constants for PS–THF will produce wildly wrong $M_v$ values.

### 2.1.5 Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight — Position Relative to $M_n$ and $M_w$

For the *same* polymer sample, the three averages rank as:
$$M_n \le M_v \le M_w$$

For $\alpha = 1$, $M_v$ approaches $M_w$. For most synthetic polymers where $\alpha \approx 0.7$, $M_v$ lies closer to $M_w$ than to $M_n$.

## 2.2 Theoretical Background of End Group Analysis

### 2.2.1 Principle

Linear polymers synthesised by step-growth (condensation) polymerisation possess well-defined, chemically reactive end groups. If each polymer chain contains exactly **$f$** reactive end groups (functionality), then:

$$M_n = \dfrac{f \times W}{N_{end}}$$

where $W$ is the mass of the polymer sample and $N_{end}$ is the *number of moles* of end groups titrated.

For a strictly **linear** chain-growth/condensation polymer, $f = 2$ (one end group at each terminus). Hence:

$$\boxed{M_n = \dfrac{2 \times W}{N_{end}}}$$

If the polymer is branched and the average number of end groups per chain is $f$, then use $f$ in the numerator.

### 2.2.2 Common Polymers and Their End Groups

| Polymer | End Group 1 | End Group 2 | Titration Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Polyester (from diacid + diol) | $-\text{COOH}$ | $-\text{OH}$ | Acid–base titration (NaOH, phenolphthalein) |
| Polyamide (Nylon 6,6) | $-\text{COOH}$ | $-\text{NH}_2$ | Acid titration + conductometric $\text{NH}_2$ estimation |
| Polyurethane | $-\text{OH}$ | $-\text{NCO}$ | Back-titration with dibutylamine |
| Epoxy resin (uncured) | Epoxide ring | $-\text{OH}$ | HCl–dioxane back-titration |

### 2.2.3 Worked Derivation for a Polyester Titration

Let the polyester be synthesised from a diacid and a diol. The reaction is:
$$n\,\text{HOOC–R–COOH} + n\,\text{HO–R'–OH} \longrightarrow \text{H–[OOC–R–COO–R'–O]}_n\text{–H} + (2n - 1)\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Each chain has two terminal groups: one $-\text{COOH}$ and one $-\text{OH}$. If we titrate the acidic end groups against standardised NaOH:

- Let normality of NaOH = $N$ (eq/L)
- Let volume of NaOH consumed = $V$ mL
- Then equivalents of $-\text{COOH}$ = $N \times V \times 10^{-3}$
- Moles of $-\text{COOH}$ = $N \times V \times 10^{-3}$ (since each $-\text{COOH}$ supplies 1 H⁺)
- Since each chain has *one* $-\text{COOH}$, moles of chains = moles of $-\text{COOH}$

If $W$ grams of polyester are taken:
$$M_n = \dfrac{W}{\text{moles of chains}} = \dfrac{W}{N \times V \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{1000 \, W}{N \, V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** For polyesters titrating *only* the acid ends, the numerator carries $f = 1$ (not 2), because only one type of end group is being titrated and there is exactly *one* such group per chain.

### 2.2.4 General End Group Formula

$$\boxed{M_n = \dfrac{W \times 1000}{N \times V \times f}}$$

where:
- $W$ = mass of polymer sample (g)
- $N$ = normality of titrant
- $V$ = volume of titrant (mL)
- $f$ = number of end groups of the *titrated* type per polymer chain

If the same end group exists twice per chain (e.g., diamine ends in some polyamides), $f$ for that end group = 2.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{rel} = \dfrac{t}{t_0}$ | Relative viscosity | From flow times |
| 2 | $\eta_{sp} = \eta_{rel} - 1$ | Specific viscosity | Fractional increase |
| 3 | $\eta_{red} = \dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c}$ | Reduced viscosity | Plot vs $c$ |
| 4 | $\eta_{inh} = \dfrac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c}$ | Inherent viscosity | Plot vs $c$ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_H[\eta]^2 c$ | Huggins | y-intercept gives $[\eta]$ |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c} = [\eta] + k_K[\eta]^2 c$ | Kraemer | y-intercept gives $[\eta]$ |
| 7 | $[\eta] = K M_v^{\alpha}$ | Mark–Houwink | Calculate $M_v$ |
| 8 | $M_v = \left( \dfrac{[\eta]}{K} \right)^{1/\alpha}$ | Rearranged | Direct calculation |
| 9 | $M_n = \dfrac{2W}{N_{end}}$ | End group, $f=2$ | Linear polymer |
| 10 | $M_n = \dfrac{1000 \, W}{N \, V}$ | Titration form | When $f=1$ |
| 11 | $M_n = \dfrac{1000 \, W}{N \, V \, f}$ | General end group | All cases |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Industry Cares:**
> 1. **Quality control in polymer manufacturing** – $M_v$ is fast to determine and correlates with mechanical strength (e.g., PVC pipes, PET bottles).
> 2. **Process optimisation** – End group analysis helps monitor the progress of condensation polymerisation in real time (e.g., Nylon-6,6 production in batch reactors).
> 3. **Drug delivery polymers** – $M_n$ of PLA/PLGA determines degradation kinetics; end group analysis is critical for quality control in biomedical-grade polymers.
> 4. **Recycling industry** – Viscosity-based "melt flow index" (MFI) is the industry-standard QC test, conceptually similar to the lab viscosity method.
