## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended Attempt**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]**

(a) **Explain with balanced chemical equations the principle and procedure for the determination of Iodine Value by the Wijs method.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** Iodine Value is determined by reacting the unsaturated polymer with a known excess of **iodine monochloride (ICl)** dissolved in glacial acetic acid (Wijs reagent). ICl adds across each C=C bond by electrophilic addition. The amount of ICl that reacts is proportional to the unsaturation. The unreacted ICl is determined by liberating free I₂ via excess KI and back-titrating with standardised Na₂S₂O₃.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Weigh 0.1–0.5 g of accurately weighed, finely cut rubber sample into a 250 mL iodine flask.
2. Add 25 mL of Wijs reagent (ICl in glacial acetic acid).
3. Swirl gently and store in the **dark for 30 minutes** at 25 ± 2 °C (to prevent photodecomposition of ICl).
4. Add 20 mL of 10 % KI solution and 100 mL distilled water. The unreacted ICl is converted to free I₂ (brown colour).
5. Titrate the liberated I₂ with **0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃** until a pale yellow colour appears.
6. Add 1–2 mL of 1 % starch indicator; the solution turns deep blue.
7. Continue the titration dropwise until the blue colour just disappears. Note the burette reading S.
8. Run a blank determination (no sample) under identical conditions. Note the burette reading B.

**Balanced chemical equations (2 marks):**

$$
\text{>C=C<} + \text{ICl} \longrightarrow \text{>CI-CCl<}
$$

$$
\text{ICl} + \text{KI} \longrightarrow \text{KCl} + \text{I}_{2}
$$

$$
2\text{Na}_{2}\text{S}_{2}\text{O}_{3} + \text{I}_{2} \longrightarrow \text{Na}_{2}\text{S}_{4}\text{O}_{6} + 2\text{NaI}
$$

---

(b) **In the determination of the iodine value of a natural rubber sample, the following data were obtained:**
- Mass of sample: **W = 0.2150 g**
- Normality of Na₂S₂O₃: **N = 0.05 N**
- Blank reading: **B = 49.80 mL**
- Sample reading: **S = 38.40 mL**

**Calculate the iodine value and comment on the result.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks — step-by-step):**

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$
\text{I.V.} = \frac{(B - S) \times N \times 12.69}{W}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\text{I.V.} = \frac{(49.80 - 38.40) \times 0.05 \times 12.69}{0.2150}
$$

**Step 3 — Arithmetic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{I.V.} &= \frac{11.40 \times 0.05 \times 12.69}{0.2150} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{11.40 \times 0.6345}{0.2150} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7.2333}{0.2150} \\[4pt]
&= 33.64 \text{ g I}_{2} \text{ per 100 g of sample}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment (3 marks for inference):**
A value of 33.64 g I₂/100 g is **far below** the expected I.V. of fresh NR (≈ 350 g/100 g). This drastic reduction indicates that the natural rubber has been **extensively vulcanized** — every cross-link consumes one C=C, and a typical sulphur-cured NR has only 1–5 % of its original double bonds remaining in a free, unreacted state. Alternatively, the sample may be a **blend** with a saturated or low-unsaturation polymer (e.g., EPDM, IIR). The I.V. test therefore confirms a *cured* or *blended* compound.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write the formula but forget the *factor 12.69*. The factor itself derives from 126.91/10 — if the examiner demands the *derivation*, you must show:
> (B−S)·N = mEq of I₂; × 126.91 mg = mass of I₂ in mg; ÷ W = mg per g; × 100/1000 = g per 100 g → final factor = N × 12.691. **Loss of this derivation = 1 mark deducted.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]**

(a) **Describe the determination of carbon black content in a vulcanized rubber compound by the pyrolysis-combustion method. Give the relevant formulae.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** The rubber sample is first heated in an *inert atmosphere* (N₂) at 500–600 °C. Under these conditions, the polymer matrix, plasticizers, oils, and any organic additives decompose and volatilize completely, leaving behind carbon black plus the inorganic ash. In a second step, the carbon black is burnt off in *air/oxygen* at 800–900 °C to leave only the inorganic ash. The mass lost in the second step equals the mass of carbon black.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Weigh an empty porcelain/silica crucible (W_c) accurately to 0.1 mg.
2. Add ≈ 0.5–1.0 g of the rubber sample, weigh again (W_s). Sample mass = W_s − W_c.
3. Place the crucible in a muffle furnace / tube furnace. Pass N₂ gas. Heat to 550 °C for 1 hour. Allow to cool in N₂ atmosphere, then weigh (W_p). This residue = carbon black + ash.
4. Switch the gas to air (or O₂). Re-heat the same crucible to 850 °C for 2 hours. The carbon black is burnt off as CO₂. Cool in a desiccator, weigh (W_a). This residue = ash only.
5. Calculate the carbon black and ash percentages using the formulae below.

**Formulae (2 marks):**

$$
\%\text{Carbon Black} = \frac{W_{p} - W_{a}}{W_{s} - W_{c}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{Ash (Total Inorganic)} = \frac{W_{a} - W_{c}}{W_{s} - W_{c}} \times 100
$$

---

(b) **In a carbon black determination, the following data were recorded:**
- Mass of empty crucible, W_c = **32.1542 g**
- Mass of crucible + sample, W_s = **32.7342 g**
- Mass of crucible after pyrolysis (N₂, 550 °C), W_p = **32.2854 g**
- Mass of crucible after combustion (air, 850 °C), W_a = **32.2468 g**

**Calculate the percentage of (i) Carbon Black, (ii) Total Inorganic (Ash) content, and (iii) Polymer content of the compound.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks — step-by-step):**

**Step 1 — Mass of sample:**

$$
W_{\text{sample}} = W_s - W_c = 32.7342 - 32.1542 = 0.5800 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of carbon black + ash (after pyrolysis):**

$$
W_{CB + \text{Ash}} = W_p - W_c = 32.2854 - 32.1542 = 0.1312 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of ash only (after combustion):**

$$
W_{\text{Ash}} = W_a - W_c = 32.2468 - 32.1542 = 0.0926 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of carbon black alone:**

$$
W_{CB} = W_{CB + \text{Ash}} - W_{\text{Ash}} = 0.1312 - 0.0926 = 0.0386 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage calculations (consolidated):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Carbon Black} &= \frac{W_{p} - W_{a}}{W_{s} - W_{c}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{32.2854 - 32.2468}{32.7342 - 32.1542} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.0386}{0.5800} \times 100 = 6.66\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Ash} &= \frac{W_{a} - W_{c}}{W_{s} - W_{c}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.0926}{0.5800} \times 100 = 15.97\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Polymer (approx.)} &= 100 - \%\text{CB} - \%\text{Ash} \\[4pt]
&= 100 - 6.66 - 15.97 = 77.37\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Comments on values (extra marks for inference):**
- 6.66 % carbon black is a *light filler loading* — typical of non-tyre, low-abrasion articles (e.g., gaskets, hoses).
- 15.97 % ash is *moderate* — consistent with a ZnO + whiting (CaCO₃) + minor silica formulation.
- 77.37 % polymer is reasonable for a quality rubber compound.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Carbon Black Test:** The single most common mistake in this numerical is **using the wrong reference mass in the denominator**. The denominator must be the *original sample mass* (W_s − W_c), not (W_p − W_c) or (W_a − W_c). If a student writes:
> $$\%\text{CB} = \frac{W_p - W_a}{W_p - W_c} \times 100$$
> this is **wrong** and will cost 2 marks. Always double-check the denominator against the principle.

---

## 5.3 Supplementary Short Numerical Set (For Practice)

> [!NOTE]
> These are rapid-fire practice problems. Solve them as a self-test before the ESE.

**P1.** A rubber sample weighing 0.200 g consumed 18.5 mL of 0.05 N Na₂S₂O₃. Blank reading = 35.0 mL. Find Iodine Value.  
*Answer:* I.V. = (35.0 − 18.5) × 0.05 × 12.69 / 0.200 = 52.36 g I₂/100 g.

**P2.** A sample of 0.500 g gave crucible masses: W_c = 28.1000, W_s = 28.6000, W_p = 28.1850, W_a = 28.1625 g. Find % CB and % Ash.  
*Answer:* W_sample = 0.5000 g; W_CB = 0.0225 g → %CB = 4.50 %; W_Ash = 0.0625 g → %Ash = 12.50 %; %Polymer ≈ 83.0 %.

**P3.** In the sodium sulphite method, 1.5 g of rubber gave a thiosulphate titre equivalent to 4.8 mL of 0.05 N I₂. Find % Free S.  
*Answer:* %S = (4.8 × 0.05 × 32 × 100) / (2 × 1.5 × 1000) = 0.256 %.

**P4.** A 0.8500 g rubber sample gave an ash mass of 0.1235 g. If the ash after HF treatment left 0.0852 g residue, find % Silica in the compound.  
*Answer:* W_sample = 0.8500 g; W_ash = 0.1235 g; W_post-HF = 0.0852 g; W_SiO₂ = 0.0383 g → %SiO₂ = 4.51 %.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls)

> [!WARNING]
> **Five common mark-losing mistakes to avoid in the ESE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ash with carbon black.** Ash is what remains *after* complete combustion; carbon black is the *combustible* residue that disappears in air. Many students write the same formula for both.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong blank in the Iodine Value calculation.** The blank is the Wijs reagent titrated *without* sample — its reading is *larger* than the sample reading. Forgetting to subtract (B − S) gives a wrong, large value.
>
> 3. **Omitting the dark incubation step in Wijs method.** The 30-min dark storage is *mandatory* because ICl is photosensitive. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark if not mentioned.
>
> 4. **Reporting silica in the wrong units.** Silica is reported as *wt % of the original compound*, not as % of the ash. State both for clarity.
>
> 5. **HF safety.** Any answer that uses HF *without* explicitly mentioning "Pt crucible", "fume hood", and "PPE" will lose 1 mark in the silica question. The KTU board considers safety as a co-equal learning outcome.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision before the ESE — go through this list mentally.**

- [ ] **Iodine Value** = grams of I₂ absorbed per 100 g of sample. Formula: (B − S)·N·12.69 / W. Method: Wijs / Hanus. Indicates unsaturation.
- [ ] **Carbon Black** is *elemental carbon* — invisible to the ash test; it is measured by **thermogravimetry** (pyrolysis in N₂ + combustion in O₂). Formula: (Wₚ − Wₐ) × 100 / W_sample.
- [ ] **Free Sulphur** is the unreacted S that causes bloom. Method: Na₂SO₃ (sodium sulphite) reaction → forms thiosulphate → titrated with I₂. Formula: V·N·M_S·100 / (n·W·1000).
- [ ] **Total Inorganic Content (Ash)** = mass left after muffle furnace combustion. Formula: (W_a − W_c) × 100 / W_sample. Temperature matters: 550 °C preserves carbonates; 900 °C converts them to oxides.
- [ ] **Silica (SiO₂)** is the *only* common inorganic filler that is *volatile* in HF. Treat ash with HF in a *platinum* crucible → SiF₄↑. Loss in mass = SiO₂.
- [ ] **HF is dangerous** — always fume hood, Pt crucible, PPE. KTU board checks this.
- [ ] **Internal choice rule (ESE Part B)**: always answer either (a) OR (b), not both. Pick the one you can answer fully — partial answers score 0.
- [ ] **Units in the final answer must be g I₂/100 g for iodine value, and wt % for the other four tests.**
- [ ] **Standard reference methods to memorize**: Iodine Value → ASTM D2075 / D1959; Carbon Black → ASTM D1603 (modified); Free Sulphur → ASTM D297 (Section 16); Ash → ASTM D297 (Section 10); Silica → ASTM D297 (Section 33).
- [ ] **Polymer identification by I.V. (memory aid)**: NR ≈ 350; SBR ≈ 170; BR ≈ 450; NBR ≈ 250–400 (depends on acrylonitrile); IIR ≈ 10; EPDM < 30. (Use only as a rough guide.)
- [ ] **Starch indicator** is added near the endpoint (when the yellow colour of I₂ has nearly faded), *not* at the start. Adding it to a deep brown I₂ solution gives a near-irreversible blue complex.
- [ ] **Crucible constant mass** — always ignite, cool in desiccator, weigh to ± 0.0001 g, repeat until two consecutive weighings differ by < 0.0002 g, before recording W_c, W_s, W_p, W_a.
- [ ] **Polymer content** is computed *by difference* only if oil/plasticizer are negligible. If oil is present, the oil content must be determined separately by Soxhlet extraction and subtracted first.
- [ ] **SI units throughout** — report masses in grams (with 4 decimal places), volumes in mL (with 2 decimal places), normality with the number of significant figures given in the question.

---

*End of Module Experiment 2 — Analysis of Polymer Compounds.*
*PCPOL407 | KTU 2024 Scheme | Polymer Preparation and Analysis Lab*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identification of Rubbers: NR, EPDM, Hypalon, Thiokol, and Silicone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL407 - POLYMER PREPARATION AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Identification of Rubbers: NR, EPDM, Hypalon, Thiokol, and Silicone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rubber Identification (KTU 2024 – PCPOL407 Module 2):**
> *Rubber identification* is the systematic qualitative analysis of an unknown elastomeric sample using a battery of physico-chemical tests — including specific gravity determination, **Liebig's burning test**, **solubility profiling**, **elemental detection (Beilstein/Lassaigne's tests)**, and **FTIR spectroscopy** — to conclusively classify the elastomer as Natural Rubber (NR), Ethylene Propylene Diene Monomer (EPDM), Chlorosulfonated Polyethylene (Hypalon), Polysulfide (Thiokol), or Polydimethylsiloxane (Silicone rubber).

The procedure relies on the fact that each elastomer possesses a **unique fingerprint** arising from its repeat-unit chemistry, which directly governs its density, flame behaviour, swelling response in selective solvents, and characteristic IR absorption bands.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Fingerprinting a Polymer"**
> Imagine five strangers standing in a dark room. You cannot see their faces, but you can *smell* their perfume, *feel* the weight of a handshake, *watch* how their clothes react to a spark, and *dip* a corner of their shawl in water to see if it soaks through. By combining all these "secondary clues," you uniquely identify each person. **Rubber identification works the same way** — no single test is conclusive, but a *battery* of small qualitative tests forms a unique signature for each elastomer.

### 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Relevance:** Rubber compounding engineers must positively identify incoming raw elastomers before blending, because cure systems (sulfur vs. peroxide), accelerator packages, and filler choices are **elastomer-specific**. A misidentified Hypalon mixed into an NR recipe will fail vulcanisation. This experiment trains the student in **failure-analysis thinking** — a core polymer-engineering competency mapped to **CO2: Analyse the physical and chemical properties of polymers.**

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics Used

The following standard reference values are used throughout this experiment:

- **Standard laboratory temperature:** $\mathbf{27^{\circ}C \pm 2^{\circ}C}$ (as per KTU lab manual)
- **Density of water at 27 °C:** $\mathbf{\rho_{H_2O} = 0.9965 \; g \cdot cm^{-3}}$
- **Beilstein flame-test threshold for Cl:** as low as **0.05 µg of chlorine** detectable
- **FTIR spectral range scanned:** **4000 – 400 cm⁻¹** (mid-IR region)
- **Solubility parameter reference solvent (toluene):** $\mathbf{\delta_{toluene} \approx 18.2 \; MPa^{1/2}}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Theory — The "Why" Behind Each Test

#### 2.1.1 Specific Gravity (Density) Test

A polymer's density is governed by its **molar mass of repeat unit ($M_0$)** and the **van der Waals volume ($V_w$ of repeat unit)**, expressed through the *molar volume*:

$$\rho = \frac{M_0}{N_A \cdot V_w + V_{free}}$$

where $V_{free}$ is the free volume. **Heavier atoms (Cl, S, Si) in the backbone raise density**, while hydrocarbon-only backbones (NR, EPDM) are lighter. This is the *first* differentiating parameter.

#### 2.1.2 Liebig's Burning Test (Flame Test)

When a polymer is heated in a flame, its backbone decomposes to give **volatile pyrolysates** whose flame colour, smoke, and smell are diagnostic:

- **NR** → Isoprene monomer vapour → **Yellow, sooty flame**, *strong burnt-rubber odour*.
- **EPDM** → Aliphatic hydrocarbon fragments → **Pale blue, non-luminous flame**, *paraffin-like (candle) odour*.
- **Hypalon** → Releases **HCl** (due to -Cl groups) and **SO₂** (due to -SO₂Cl) → **Self-extinguishing green-edged flame**, *pungent acidic odour*.
- **Thiokol** → Releases **H₂S, SO₂** from -S-S- cleavage → **Blue flame with brimstone odour**.
- **Silicone** → SiO₂ micro-particulates in flame → **Bright white flame, dense white smoke (SiO₂ ash)**, *no carbon soot*.

#### 2.1.3 Solubility Profiling

A polymer dissolves in a solvent when their **Hildebrand solubility parameters ($\delta$)** are within $\pm 2 \; MPa^{1/2}$. The *Flory-Huggins interaction parameter* $\chi$ quantifies this:

$$\chi = \frac{V_s}{RT}\left(\delta_p - \delta_s\right)^2$$

where $V_s$ is the molar volume of the solvent, $R$ is the gas constant, $T$ is absolute temperature, and subscripts *p* and *s* denote polymer and solvent respectively. A **low $\chi$** means good miscibility.

#### 2.1.4 Beilstein's Test (for Halogens — Cl in Hypalon)

A copper wire is heated in a Bunsen flame until the flame is colourless, then dipped in the sample and reheated. **Organochlorine compounds** decompose to give **CuCl** which volatilises and imparts a **bright green / bluish-green flame**. This test is *positive* for Hypalon and *negative* for NR, EPDM, Thiokol, and Silicone.

#### 2.1.5 FTIR Fingerprint Region

Each functional group absorbs at a characteristic wavenumber:

- **NR:** $\mathbf{835 \; cm^{-1}}$ (C=CH out-of-plane bend of *cis*-1,4-isoprene), $\mathbf{1665 \; cm^{-1}}$ (C=C stretch).
- **EPDM:** $\mathbf{2920 \; cm^{-1}}$ and $\mathbf{2850 \; cm^{-1}}$ (C-H stretch, saturated), no C=C near 1665.
- **Hypalon:** $\mathbf{1370 \; cm^{-1}}$ (SO₂ asymmetric stretch), $\mathbf{1160 \; cm^{-1}}$ (SO₂ symmetric), C-Cl at $\mathbf{600-700 \; cm^{-1}}$.
- **Thiokol:** $\mathbf{500-700 \; cm^{-1}}$ (C-S stretch), $\mathbf{1050 \; cm^{-1}}$ (S-S stretch).
- **Silicone:** $\mathbf{1000-1100 \; cm^{-1}}$ (Si-O-Si asymmetric stretch, very broad & strong), $\mathbf{1260 \; cm^{-1}}$ (Si-CH₃ symmetric bend), absence of C=O and O-H.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **high-yield KTU reference card** — memorise it for the 2-mark short-answer questions in the University ESE.

| **Parameter** | **NR** | **EPDM** | **Hypalon (CSM)** | **Thiokol** | **Silicone** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Chemical class** | *cis*-1,4-polyisoprene | Ethylene-Propylene-Diene terpolymer | Chlorosulfonated polyethylene | Polysulfide (-(R-S-S)ₙ-) | Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) |
| **Repeat unit** | $\text{-CH}_2\text{-C(CH}_3\text{)=CH-CH}_2\text{-}$ | $\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH(CH}_3\text{)-CH}_2\text{-}$ | $\text{-(CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{)}_n$ with Cl & SO₂Cl | $\text{-R-S-S-}$ | $\text{-Si(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{-O-}$ |
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 0.91 – 0.93 | 0.86 – 0.87 | 1.10 – 1.28 | 1.25 – 1.35 | 0.95 – 1.05 (filled: 1.10 – 1.50) |
| **Flame colour** | Yellow, sooty | Pale blue, non-luminous | Green-edged, self-extinguishing | Blue with yellow tip | Bright white, dense white smoke |
| **Characteristic odour** | Burnt rubber / isoprene | Paraffin / candle wax | Pungent HCl + SO₂ | Brimstone / rotten egg (H₂S) | None (slight sweet/formaldehyde) |
| **Solubility in toluene** | Soluble (swells then dissolves) | Swells slightly | Insoluble | Insoluble | Insoluble |
| **Sol. in acetone** | Swells | Insoluble | Swells | Insoluble | Swells |
| **Beilstein test (Cl)** | Negative | Negative | **Positive (green flame)** | Negative | Negative |
| **Distinguishing element** | C, H only | C, H only | C, H, **Cl, S** | C, H, **S** | C, H, O, **Si** |
| **Key FTIR band (cm⁻¹)** | 835 (C=CH bend) | 2920 (CH stretch) | 1370 (SO₂ asym) | 1050 (S-S) | 1010 (Si-O-Si asym) |
| **$\delta$ (MPa¹ᐟ²)** | ≈ 16.6 | ≈ 16.2 | ≈ 19.0 | ≈ 19.2 | ≈ 15.0 – 16.5 |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Application of This Identification Skill:**
> - **Tyre industry** (uses NR, SBR, BR — must not be confused with EPDM during compounding).
> - **Automotive sealing** (EPDM — weather strips, Hypalon — hose covers).
> - **Aerospace sealants** (Thiokol LP-32 polysulfide fuel-tank sealants).
> - **Medical implants & high-temp gaskets** (Silicone — USP Class VI).
> - **Wire & cable insulation** (Hypalon for flame retardancy).
>
> A *single mis-identification* at the receiving dock can cost an industry up to **₹5–10 lakhs** in scrapped batches.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Gravity by Archimedes' Principle

The density of an elastomer is determined by weighing the sample in air ($W_a$) and in water ($W_w$). The specific gravity is then computed as follows:

$$\text{Specific Gravity (S.G.)} = \frac{W_a}{W_a - W_w}$$

The actual density is then obtained by multiplying with the density of water at the test temperature:

$$\rho_{polymer} = S.G. \times \rho_{H_2O \; at \; 27^{\circ}C}$$

For example, with $W_a = 2.83 \; g$, $W_w = 1.76 \; g$, and $\rho_{H_2O} = 0.9965 \; g/cm^3$:

**Step 1:** Substitute into the numerator-denominator:

$$S.G. = \frac{2.83}{2.83 - 1.76}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the denominator:

$$S.G. = \frac{2.83}{1.07}$$

**Step 3:** Carry out the division:

$$S.G. = 2.6449$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by water density:

$$\rho_{polymer} = 2.6449 \times 0.9965 = 2.6356 \; g \cdot cm^{-3}$$

**Interpretation:** A density of $\mathbf{2.64 \; g \cdot cm^{-3}}$ is anomalously high for an elastomer; this value indicates the sample likely contains **metallic fillers (e.g., barium sulfate, zinc oxide)**. A pure unfilled rubber would not exceed $\mathbf{1.5 \; g \cdot cm^{-3}}$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Solubility Parameter Approximation via Group Contribution (Hoftyzer–van Krevelen)

For an unknown elastomer, the *Hildebrand solubility parameter* can be estimated using the additive **group contribution method**:

$$\delta = \left[\frac{\sum F_i}{V_m}\right] \quad (in \; MPa^{1/2} \; equivalent \; \cdot \text{conversion})$$

where $F_i$ is the molar-attraction constant of group *i* (in $J^{1/2} \cdot cm^{3/2} \cdot mol^{-1}$) and $V_m$ is the molar volume of the repeat unit (in $cm^3 \cdot mol^{-1}$). The dimensional conversion is:

$$\delta \; (MPa^{1/2}) = \frac{\left[\dfrac{\sum F_i}{V_m}\right]}{1}$$

**Worked Example — Hypalon (CSM, repeat unit –CH₂–CH₂– with 1 Cl and 1 SO₂Cl per 7 ethylene units, simplified as –CH₂–CHCl–CH₂–CH(SO₂Cl)– for illustration):**

| **Group** | **Count** | $F_i$ (J¹ᐟ² cm³ᐟ² mol⁻¹) | **Contribution** |
|---|---|---|---|
| –CH₂– | 2 | 269 | 538 |
| –CHCl– | 1 | 492 | 492 |
| –CH(SO₂Cl)– | 1 | (combined) | 720 |
| **Total** | — | — | $\sum F_i = 1750$ |

Then $V_m \approx 78 \; cm^3/mol$ (from density data), giving:

**Step 1:** Divide:

$$\delta \approx \frac{1750}{78}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate:

$$\delta \approx 22.4 \; MPa^{1/2}$$

**Note:** The experimentally reported value for Hypalon is $\mathbf{\approx 19.0 \; MPa^{1/2}}$; the divergence comes from assumptions in group contribution for the polar sulfonyl chloride group. For KTU exam purposes, the **experimentally tabulated $\delta$ value is accepted** without re-derivation.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Flory–Huggins Interaction Parameter

When a polymer is placed in a solvent of molar volume $V_s$, the Flory–Huggins interaction parameter $\chi$ controls swelling:

$$\chi = \frac{V_s}{RT} \left(\delta_p - \delta_s\right)^2$$

**Step 1:** For NR ($\delta_p = 16.6 \; MPa^{1/2}$) in toluene ($\delta_s = 18.2 \; MPa^{1/2}$, $V_s = 106.3 \; cm^3/mol$, $R = 8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$, $T = 300 \; K$):

$$\Delta \delta = 16.6 - 18.2 = -1.6 \; MPa^{1/2}$$

**Step 2:** Square the difference:

$$(\Delta \delta)^2 = 2.56 \; (MPa^{1/2})^2$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by $V_s$:

$$V_s \cdot (\Delta \delta)^2 = 106.3 \times 2.56 = 272.13$$

**Step 4:** Divide by $RT$:

$$\chi = \frac{272.13}{8.314 \times 300} = \frac{272.13}{2494.2} = 0.109$$

**Step 5:** Interpret: Since $\mathbf{\chi < 0.5}$, NR is **thermodynamically miscible** with toluene at 300 K — explaining why NR *dissolves* (not merely swells) in toluene. This single calculation predicts the solubility observation.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Master Identification Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following decision matrix is the *master analytical key* for KTU practical exams. Use it during the exam when a sample is given.

| **Step** | **Test Performed** | **Observation** | **Conclusion → Next Test** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Visual + feel | Soft, elastic, tacky | Probable NR or EPDM |
| 1 | Visual + feel | Dry, leathery, no tack | Probable Hypalon, Thiokol, or Silicone |
| 2 | Density (Archimedes) | $\rho < 0.95 \; g \cdot cm^{-3}$ | → NR or EPDM |
| 2 | Density (Archimedes) | $\rho > 1.10 \; g \cdot cm^{-3}$ | → Hypalon, Thiokol, or filled Silicone |
| 2 | Density (Archimedes) | $\rho \approx 1.0 \; g \cdot cm^{-3}$ | → Unfilled Silicone |
| 3 | Burning test (Liebig) | Yellow sooty + burnt-rubber smell | → **NR** ✓ |
| 3 | Burning test (Liebig) | Blue non-luminous + paraffin smell | → **EPDM** ✓ |
| 3 | Burning test (Liebig) | Green-edged, self-extinguishing + HCl | → **Hypalon** ✓ |
| 3 | Burning test (Liebig) | Blue flame + brimstone (rotten egg) smell | → **Thiokol** ✓ |
| 3 | Burning test (Liebig) | Bright white flame + white SiO₂ smoke | → **Silicone** ✓ |
| 4 | Beilstein (Cu wire) | Green flame | **Confirms Cl** → Hypalon |
| 4 | Solubility in toluene | Dissolves | Confirms NR |
| 4 | Solubility in toluene | Swells only | Confirms EPDM |
| 5 | FTIR (final confirmation) | Match fingerprint peaks | Final identification |

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Mass% Composition from Density

A rubber sample weighs 1.20 g in air and 0.50 g when immersed in water at 27 °C. Identify which rubber it could be (assume no filler).

**Step 1:** Compute specific gravity:

$$S.G. = \frac{1.20}{1.20 - 0.50} = \frac{1.20}{0.70} = 1.7143$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by water density:

$$\rho = 1.7143 \times 0.9965 = 1.7083 \; g \cdot cm^{-3}$$

**Step 3:** Match against table — this $\mathbf{1.71 \; g \cdot cm^{-3}}$ value lies **between Hypalon (1.10–1.28) and filled Silicone (1.10–1.50)**. Since Hypalon's upper limit is 1.28, the value 1.71 is **too high for pure Hypalon**. The sample is most likely a **highly filled Hypalon or Silicone** (with heavy fillers like ZnO or SiO₂). Proceed to **Beilstein test** — if positive, it is **Hypalon with filler**; if negative, it is **filled Silicone**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Identification Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Unknown Rubber Sample] --> B{Visual & Tactile Inspection}
    B -- Soft, tacky, light --> C[Probable NR or EPDM]
    B -- Dry, leathery, dark --> D[Probable Hypalon, Thiokol, Silicone]
    C --> E[Density Test by Archimedes]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Density g per cm3}
    F -- less than 0.95 --> G[NR or EPDM branch]
    F -- 0.95 to 1.10 --> H[Unfilled Silicone]
    F -- greater than 1.10 --> I[Hypalon, Thiokol, or Filled Rubber]
    G --> J[Liebig Burning Test]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Flame colour and odour}
    K -- Yellow sooty and burnt rubber --> L1[Confirmed NR]
    K -- Pale blue and paraffin --> L2[Confirmed EPDM]
    K -- Green edged and HCl --> L3[Confirmed Hypalon]
    K -- Blue and brimstone H2S --> L4[Confirmed Thiokol]
    K -- Bright white and SiO2 smoke --> L5[Confirmed Silicone]
    L1 --> M[Optional FTIR Final Check]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
    L4 --> M
    L5 --> M
    M --> N[Report: Polymer Identity, Grade, Purity]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
> *List any three characteristic observations that distinguish Hypalon (CSM) rubber from Natural Rubber (NR) during a Liebig burning test.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Flame colour:** Hypalon burns with a **green-edged flame** (due to volatile CuCl-like emission from chlorine), while NR burns with a **yellow sooty flame** — **(1 mark)**.
2. **Self-extinguishing behaviour:** Hypalon is **self-extinguishing** when removed from the flame (due to HCl release, which displaces oxygen); NR **continues to burn** with a smoky flame — **(1 mark)**.
3. **Smell of pyrolysates:** Hypalon emits a **pungent acidic (HCl + SO₂) odour**; NR emits a **characteristic burnt-rubber (isoprene) smell** — **(1 mark)**.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
> *Give the chemical class and the key FTIR absorption band (with wavenumber) for silicone rubber.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Chemical class:** Silicone rubber belongs to the class of **polyorganosiloxanes**, with the most common member being **polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)** having the repeat unit $-\text{Si(CH}_3)_2\text{-O-}$ — **(1.5 marks)**.
- **Key FTIR band:** A very strong, broad absorption at $\mathbf{1000 \; to \; 1100 \; cm^{-1}}$ corresponding to the **Si–O–Si asymmetric stretching vibration**, with a characteristic sharp symmetric bending of Si–CH₃ at $\mathbf{1260 \; cm^{-1}}$ — **(1.5 marks)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model — 14 Marks, Internal Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Describe, with a neat flowchart, the systematic laboratory procedure to identify an unknown rubber sample as **either NR, EPDM, Hypalon, Thiokol, or Silicone**. State the specific gravity and flame-test observation for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A rubber sample has the following properties: density $\mathbf{1.32 \; g \cdot cm^{-3}}$, on burning it produces a **blue flame with a strong rotten-egg (H₂S) smell** and leaves a **black residue**. Beilstein test is **negative**. Identify the rubber and justify with two confirmatory tests. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q3A(a):**

The systematic procedure is summarised by the **five-level test hierarchy** (Section 4.3 above). The complete procedure is:

**Step 1 — Visual & tactile:** Observe colour, surface gloss, and tackiness. NR and EPDM are tacky and light-coloured; Hypalon, Thiokol, Silicone are dry — **(1 mark)**.

**Step 2 — Specific gravity (Archimedes):** Weigh in air ($W_a$) and water ($W_w$). Compute $S.G. = W_a / (W_a - W_w)$, then $\rho = S.G. \times 0.9965$ — **(1 mark)**.

| **Rubber** | **Density (g/cm³)** | **Flame Observation** |
|---|---|---|
| NR | 0.91 – 0.93 | Yellow, sooty, burnt-rubber smell |
| EPDM | 0.86 – 0.87 | Pale blue, non-luminous, paraffin smell |
| Hypalon | 1.10 – 1.28 | Green-edged, self-extinguishing, HCl odour |
| Thiokol | 1.25 – 1.35 | Blue flame, brimstone/rotten-egg H₂S smell |
| Silicone | 0.95 – 1.50 | Bright white flame, white SiO₂ smoke |

— **(3 marks)** for the table.

**Step 3 — Liebig burning test:** Heat a small piece on a spatula and observe flame colour, soot, smell — **(1 mark)**.

**Step 4 — Beilstein (for Cl):** Heat a Cu wire, dip in sample, re-heat. **Green flame = Cl present = Hypalon** — **(0.5 marks)**.

**Step 5 — FTIR confirmation:** Match fingerprint bands (835 cm⁻¹ for NR, 1010 cm⁻¹ for Si–O–Si, etc.) — **(0.5 marks)**.

**Model Solution for Q3A(b):**

**Identification:** The sample is **Thiokol (polysulfide rubber)** — **(2 marks)**.

**Justification from the three observations:**

1. **Density $1.32 \; g \cdot cm^{-3}$** lies in the Thiokol range (1.25 – 1.35) — **(1.5 marks)**.
2. **Blue flame with rotten-egg (H₂S) smell** is the *signature* of polysulfide —S—S— bond cleavage, releasing H₂S — **(1.5 marks)**.
3. **Beilstein negative** rules out Hypalon (which has Cl and would give a green flame) — **(1 mark)**.

**Two confirmatory tests:**

- **Lassaigne's sodium-fusion test for sulfur:** Fuse the sample with sodium metal, extract with water, acidify, and add lead acetate. A **black precipitate of PbS** confirms sulfur — **(0.5 marks)**.
- **FTIR spectroscopy:** A diagnostic **S–S stretching band at ~1050 cm⁻¹** and **C–S stretch at 500–700 cm⁻¹** confirms the polysulfide backbone — **(0.5 marks)**.

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model — 14 Marks, Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **Beilstein test** in detail. How is it used to differentiate Hypalon from NR, EPDM, and Thiokol? Give the chemistry of the positive test. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a labelled FTIR spectrum (schematic), describe how you would distinguish **silicone rubber** from **Natural Rubber** using three absorption bands. Justify each assignment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q3B(a):**

**Principle (2 marks):** The Beilstein test detects the presence of **halogens (Cl, Br, I)** in an organic compound. When an organochlorine compound is heated on a **copper wire** in a Bunsen flame, the halogen reacts with copper to form **volatile cuprous halide** (CuCl), which imparts a characteristic **bright green / bluish-green colour** to the flame. The copper wire must first be pre-heated until it gives a *clean, colourless flame* to remove surface impurities — **(1 mark)**.

**Procedure (2 marks):**

1. Heat a copper wire in a Bunsen flame until the flame becomes non-luminous.
2. Dip the hot wire into the unknown rubber sample (small piece).
3. Re-introduce the wire into the flame and observe.
4. **Positive test:** A persistent **green or bluish-green flame** confirms the presence of chlorine.
5. **Negative test:** Flame remains blue/orange — no halogen present.

**Differentiation (2 marks):**
- **Hypalon (CSM)** contains ~25–43% Cl by mass → **Beilstein positive** (green flame).
- **NR, EPDM** contain only C and H → **negative** (no green flame).
- **Thiokol** contains C, H, S only (no halogen) → **negative**.

**Chemistry of the positive test (1 mark):**

$$\text{R–Cl} + \text{Cu} \; \xrightarrow{\Delta} \; \text{CuCl} \; (volatile) \; \uparrow \; + \; R^{\bullet}$$

$$\text{CuCl} \; \xrightarrow{flame} \; \text{green emission at } 535.5 \; nm$$

---

**Model Solution for Q3B(b):**

Three FTIR absorption bands that distinguish **silicone rubber (PDMS)** from **Natural Rubber (NR)**:

| **Wavenumber (cm⁻¹)** | **Assignment** | **Present in** |
|---|---|---|
| **1010 – 1100 (broad, very strong)** | **Si–O–Si asymmetric stretch** | **Silicone only** ✓ |
| **1260 (sharp)** | **Si–CH₃ symmetric bending** | **Silicone only** ✓ |
| **835 (strong)** | **C=CH out-of-plane bend of *cis*-1,4-isoprene** | **NR only** ✓ |

— **(4 marks)** for the table and assignments.

**Justification (3 marks):**

- The **Si–O–Si band at 1010–1100 cm⁻¹** is the *most diagnostic* — it is the **broadest and strongest** band in silicone's spectrum, and **cannot** originate from NR (which contains no Si or O in its backbone).
- The **Si–CH₃ bend at 1260 cm⁻¹** is unique to silicones because NR has no Si atoms in its methyl environment.
- The **835 cm⁻¹ band** is the *signature* of the **=C–H wag in the *cis*-1,4-isoprene unit** of NR. Silicones have no C=C double bond, so this band is **absent** in silicone rubber.

The simultaneous presence of *both* the 1010 and 1260 cm⁻¹ bands **and** the absence of the 835 cm⁻¹ band positively identifies the sample as **silicone rubber**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Density-Temperature Trap (–1 mark typical):** Students forget to multiply S.G. by $\rho_{H_2O}$ at the *test temperature*. At 27 °C, $\rho_{H_2O} = 0.9965$, **not** 1.000. A density reported as "1.10" instead of "1.097" loses a mark.
> 2. **Confusing Thiokol & Hypalon (–2 marks):** Both have S, but only Hypalon is Beilstein-positive. Always **state both** observations.
> 3. **Flame test of Silicone (–1 mark):** Many students write "Silicone burns with a yellow sooty flame" — this is **wrong**. Silicone gives a *white* flame and *white* smoke (SiO₂).
> 4. **Skipping the flowchart in Part B:** A *labelled* flowchart in 3A(a) is worth **at least 1 mark**; a plain paragraph loses it.
> 5. **FTIR bands without wavenumbers:** Always cite the **numerical cm⁻¹ value** — "Si–O–Si at 1010 cm⁻¹" scores; "Si–O–Si stretch" without a number loses half credit.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Viva & Exam Prep)**

- ✅ The **5 rubbers** to identify in this experiment are **NR, EPDM, Hypalon, Thiokol, Silicone**.
- ✅ **NR & EPDM** are *light* ($\rho < 0.95$), *hydrocarbon-only*, and Beilstein-negative.
- ✅ **Hypalon** is the *only* one that is **Beilstein-positive** (green flame from CuCl).
- ✅ **Thiokol** is identified by the **H₂S / rotten-egg smell**; it contains **sulfur but no chlorine**.
- ✅ **Silicone** is the *only* one with a **Si–O–Si backbone** (FTIR at 1010 cm⁻¹) and gives a *white flame with white SiO₂ smoke*.
- ✅ The **Flory–Huggins parameter $\chi$** explains *why* NR dissolves in toluene ($\chi < 0.5$) and why Hypalon/Thiokol do not.
- ✅ The **Archimedes density formula** is $S.G. = W_a / (W_a - W_w)$, multiplied by $\rho_{H_2O, 27°C} = 0.9965 \; g \cdot cm^{-3}$.
- ✅ Always use a **flowchart** in the 14-mark question to gain the full marks for procedure.
- ✅ **Liebig's burning test** is the *single most important* qualitative test — it can directly identify all five rubbers.
- ✅ **Beilstein's test** is *specifically* for **halogens**; positive = green flame; negative = no halogen.
- ✅ **FTIR wavenumbers to memorise:** NR → 835 cm⁻¹, EPDM → 2920 cm⁻¹, Hypalon → 1370 cm⁻¹, Thiokol → 1050 cm⁻¹, Silicone → 1010 cm⁻¹.
- ✅ **KTU CO mapped:** This experiment is aligned with **CO2** (Analyse physical and chemical properties of polymers) and **CO4** (Apply standard polymer-testing protocols).
- ✅ For a 2-mark short answer, the *best* answer lists **density + flame behaviour + one chemical test** for the asked rubber.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identification of Plastics: PE and Polyester
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOL407 - POLYMER PREPARATION AND ANALYSIS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Identification of Plastics: PE and Polyester # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Plastic Identification** refers to the systematic laboratory procedure employed to determine the chemical class, grade, and structural composition of an unknown plastic specimen using a combination of physical, chemical, thermal, and spectroscopic techniques. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCPOL407**, this experiment falls under *Module Experiment 2*, where students are expected to identify common plastics — specifically **Polyethylene (PE)** and **Polyester (PET)** — using standardized qualitative analytical methods. ### Definition of Key Polymers **Polyethylene (PE)** is a thermoplastic polymer synthesized from the monomer ethylene (CH₂=CH₂). Its repeating structural unit is $-(CH_2-CH_2)_n-$, where **n** represents the degree of polymerization. **Polyester (PET – Polyethylene Terephthalate)** is a semi-aromatic thermoplastic polyester formed by the condensation polymerization of ethylene glycol and terephthalic acid (or dimethyl terephthalate). Its repeating unit contains an **ester linkage** ($-COO-$) and an **aromatic benzene ring**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The identification of plastics relies primarily on **qualitative tests** — burning test, solubility test, density test, and confirmation via **FTIR spectroscopy**. No quantitative synthesis is required at this stage. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have two unmarked bottles — one is a **shampoo bottle** (PE) and the other is a **cold drink bottle** (PET). They look similar at first glance, but they behave very differently: | Feature | Shampoo Bottle (PE) | Cold Drink Bottle (PET) | |---|---|---| | Feel | Waxy, soft, squeezable | Hard, rigid, glass-like clarity | | Density | Floats on water | Sinks in water | | Burning | Blue flame, candle-wax smell | Yellow sooty flame, sweet smell | | Sound when tapped | Dull thud | Metallic ring | The lab identification procedure mimics a **detective's forensic investigation** — by applying a series of physical and chemical "questions" (tests) to the polymer sample, we narrow down its true identity. The **burning test** is the most dramatic (like a fire investigation), the **density test** is the simplest (like checking if a suspect floats or sinks in a pond), and **FTIR spectroscopy** is the final "fingerprint confirmation" — like matching a fingerprint at a crime scene. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Identification Matters in Engineering:** In the plastics recycling industry, correct identification of PE and PET determines the recycling stream — mixing them contaminates the recycled product. In failure analysis of polymer components, misidentification can lead to wrong material substitution and catastrophic engineering failures. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants - **Density of PE (LDPE):** $\approx \mathbf{0.910 - 0.925 \ g/cm^3}$ - **Density of PE (HDPE):** $\approx \mathbf{0.941 - 0.965 \ g/cm^3}$ - **Density of PET:** $\approx \mathbf{1.30 - 1.40 \ g/cm^3}$ (typically $\mathbf{1.38 \ g/cm^3}$) - **Density of water (reference):** $\mathbf{1.00 \ g/cm^3}$ at $25^\circ C$ - **Melting point of PE (LDPE):** $\approx \mathbf{105 - 115^\circ C}$ - **Melting point of PE (HDPE):** $\approx \mathbf{125 - 135^\circ C}$ - **Melting point of PET:** $\approx \mathbf{245 - 265^\circ C}$ - **Solubility Parameter of PE:** $\delta \approx \mathbf{16.0 - 16.6 \ (cal/cm^3)^{1/2}}$ - **Solubility Parameter of PET:** $\delta \approx \mathbf{21.9 \ (cal/cm^3)^{1/2}}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Identification Tests The systematic identification of PE and PET can be organized into a hierarchical sequence of tests, each providing progressive confirmation. ### 2.1.1 Preliminary Visual & Tactile Examination - **Visual Inspection:** Transparency, color, surface gloss. - **Tactile (Feel) Test:** PE feels **waxy and slippery**; PET feels **hard and smooth**. - **Sound Test:** Tapping PE produces a **dull thud**; tapping PET produces a **metallic ring**. - **Flexibility:** PE films are **highly flexible**; PET films are **rigid and brittle** when thin. ### 2.1.2 Density (Specific Gravity) Test The Archimedes principle is applied. The polymer sample is immersed in a graduated cylinder containing a density gradient column (often a mixture of **distilled water + NaCl or isopropanol + water**). **Principle:** - If $\rho_{polymer} < \rho_{water}$, the sample **floats** → indicates **PE**. - If $\rho_{polymer} > \rho_{water}$, the sample **sinks** → indicates **PET**. ### 2.1.3 Burning Test (Flame Test) A small fragment of the polymer is held in a non-luminous Bunsen flame using a crucible tong or nichrome wire. | Observation | PE | PET | |---|---|---| | Behavior in flame | Melts, drips | Melts, chars | | Flame color | **Blue flame** with yellow tip | **Yellow luminous sooty flame** | | Odor | **Paraffin / candle-wax smell** | **Sweet, aromatic smell** | | Smoke | Little white smoke | Heavy black sooty smoke | | Residue | None (clean) | Black carbonaceous char | | Self-extinguishing | No | Yes, slow | ### 2.1.4 Solubility Test A small amount of polymer is added to a series of solvents (e.g., toluene, xylene, acetone, phenol, methylene chloride, hot DMF). | Solvent | PE | PET | |---|---|---| | Toluene (hot) | **Swells / partially soluble** | Insoluble | | Acetone | Insoluble | Insoluble | | Phenol / Hot DMF | Insoluble | **Soluble** | | Methylene Chloride | Insoluble | **Soluble** | | Conc. H₂SO₄ | Slowly attacked | **Soluble (decomposes)** | ### 2.1.5 Chemical (Hydrolysis) Test for PET The ester linkage of PET is hydrolyzed using **NaOH** in hot ethylene glycol or aqueous KOH. This yields **terephthalate salt** and **ethylene glycol**. Acidification precipitates **terephthalic acid** as a white solid — a definitive confirmation. $$\text{PET} + 2\text{NaOH} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Disodium terephthalate} + \text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$$ ### 2.1.6 Spectroscopic Confirmation (FTIR) The **Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum** provides the definitive molecular fingerprint: - **PE:** Strong absorption bands at $\sim \mathbf{2920 \ cm^{-1}}$ and $\sim \mathbf{2850 \ cm^{-1}}$ (asymmetric and symmetric C–H stretching of $-CH_2-$), and $\sim \mathbf{1460 \ cm^{-1}}$ ($CH_2$ bending). No carbonyl peak. - **PET:** Characteristic strong absorption at $\sim \mathbf{1715 \ cm^{-1}}$ (C=O stretch of ester), $\sim \mathbf{1250 \ cm^{-1}}$ and $\sim \mathbf{1100 \ cm^{-1}}$ (C–O stretch), and aromatic C=C stretch at $\sim \mathbf{1570 \ cm^{-1}}$ and $\sim \mathbf{1500 \ cm^{-1}}$. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value notation inside tables (avoid the pipe symbol `|` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax). | Parameter / Equation | Formula | Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---| | Density (Archimedes) | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | $\text{g/cm}^3$ | Density test | | Specific Gravity | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{polymer}}{\rho_{water}}$ | dimensionless | Float/Sink test | | Degree of Polymerization | $DP = \dfrac{M_n}{M_o}$ | dimensionless | Chain length estimate | | Number-average Molecular Weight | $M_n = DP \times M_o$ | $\text{g/mol}$ | For PE: $M_o = 28$; For PET: $M_o = 192$ | | Melting Point Depression (for copolymer) | $\dfrac{1}{T_m} - \dfrac{1}{T_m^o} = -\dfrac{R}{\Delta H_f} X_n$ | $\text{K}^{-1}$ | Identify copolymer vs homopolymer | | FTIR Wavenumber | $\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\text{cm}^{-1}$ | Spectral position | | Beer-Lambert Law (FTIR quant.) | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | dimensionless | Quantitative FTIR | | Solubility Parameter (Hildebrand) | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta H_v - RT}{V_m}}$ | $(\text{cal/cm}^3)^{1/2}$ | Predict solvent compatibility | | Heat of Combustion (approx.) | $\Delta H_c \approx -46 \text{ MJ/kg}$ (PE); $\approx -22 \text{ MJ/kg}$ (PET) | $\text{MJ/kg}$ | Burning test energy | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Recycling Industry (Municipal Solid Waste):** Automatic sorting lines use **Near-Infrared (NIR) spectroscopy** — an extension of FTIR — to separate PET bottles from PE containers at high speed. - **Failure Analysis:** Identifying PE vs PET in a failed component tells the engineer whether to recommend a higher-density or higher-Tg material. - **Quality Control in Packaging:** Beverage manufacturers must confirm PET, not PE, for carbonated drink bottles (PE cannot withstand CO₂ pressure). - **Forensic Engineering:** In fire investigations, identification of plastic residues at the burn site indicates accelerants and source materials. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Density-Based Identification Logic The buoyancy principle provides a direct identification route. Let us derive the criterion mathematically. **Step 1 — Define buoyant force and weight.** For a polymer sample of mass $m$ and volume $V$ immersed in water of density $\rho_w$: $$F_{buoyant} = \rho_w \cdot V \cdot g$$ $$W = m \cdot g = \rho_{polymer} \cdot V \cdot g$$ **Step 2 — Apply equilibrium condition.** The sample **floats** when buoyant force $\geq$ weight: $$\rho_w \cdot V \cdot g \geq \rho_{polymer} \cdot V \cdot g$$ **Step 3 — Simplify.** Cancelling $V \cdot g$: $$\rho_w \geq \rho_{polymer}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{For PE (HDPE):} \quad \rho_{polymer} &\approx 0.95 \text{ g/cm}^3 \\ \rho_w &= 1.00 \text{ g/cm}^3 \\ \Rightarrow \rho_w &> \rho_{polymer} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PE FLOATS} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{For PET:} \quad \rho_{polymer} &\approx 1.38 \text{ g/cm}^3 \\ \rho_w &= 1.00 \text{ g/cm}^3 \\ \Rightarrow \rho_w &< \rho_{polymer} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PET SINKS} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Conclusion.** The density test alone differentiates PE from PET unambiguously. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Degree of Polymerization and Molecular Weight **For Polyethylene (PE):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Monomer unit (ethylene, } CH_2=CH_2): &\quad M_o = 2(12) + 4(1) = 28 \text{ g/mol} \\ M_n &= DP \times M_o \\ \Rightarrow DP &= \dfrac{M_n}{M_o} = \dfrac{M_n}{28} \end{aligned}$$ For commercial PE with $M_n \approx 1.4 \times 10^5$: $$DP = \dfrac{1.4 \times 10^5}{28} \approx 5000$$ **For PET:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Repeat unit } (-O-CH_2-CH_2-O-CO-C_6H_4-CO-): \\ M_o &= 2(16) + 2(12) + 4(1) + 12 + 4(12) + 2(16) \\ &= 32 + 24 + 4 + 12 + 48 + 32 = 192 \text{ g/mol} \end{aligned}$$ For PET with $M_n \approx 3.0 \times 10^4$: $$DP = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^4}{192} \approx 156$$ This explains why PET is generally **less chain-flexible** than PE. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Test Outcomes for PE vs PET > [!NOTE] > This matrix is a high-yield KTU exam tool. Memorize the bold-distinctive observation for each test. | Test | Expected Observation for PE | Expected Observation for PET | Inference | |---|---|---|---| | **Visual** | Translucent / opaque, waxy | Transparent, glass-clear | PET has aromatic ring | | **Touch** | Waxy, slippery | Hard, smooth | PE = flexible chains | | **Density test** | **Floats** ($\rho \approx 0.92$) | **Sinks** ($\rho \approx 1.38$) | Definitive | | **Burning test** | **Blue flame, paraffin smell** | **Yellow sooty flame, sweet smell** | Definitive | | **Melting point** | $105 - 135^\circ C$ | $245 - 265^\circ C$ | Aromatic PET needs more energy | | **Solubility (hot toluene)** | **Swells** | Insoluble | PE = non-polar | | **Solubility (phenol/DMF)** | Insoluble | **Soluble** | PET = polar ester | | **NaOH hydrolysis** | No reaction | **Hydrolyzes to terephthalate** | Ester linkage test | | **FTIR peak at 2920 cm⁻¹** | **Strong (C–H stretch)** | Moderate | PE has more CH₂ | | **FTIR peak at 1715 cm⁻¹** | Absent | **Strong (C=O stretch)** | Ester group unique to PET | | **FTIR peak at 725 cm⁻¹** | **Present (CH₂ rocking, n≥4)** | Absent | Long CH₂ sequence | | **Elemental (C, H only)** | Only C and H | C, H, and **O** | PET contains oxygen | --- ## 3.4 Detailed Step-by-Step: Laboratory Procedure for Identification **Step 1 — Sample Preparation.** Cut a small clean piece (~$1 \text{ cm}^2$) of the unknown plastic. Wipe with acetone to remove surface contaminants. **Step 2 — Visual & Tactile Examination.** Record color, transparency, flexibility, and tactile feel. PE feels waxy; PET feels hard. **Step 3 — Density Test.** Place the sample in a beaker of distilled water. Observe whether it **floats (PE)** or **sinks (PET)**. **Step 4 — Burning Test.** Using crucible tongs, hold a small fragment in a non-luminous Bunsen burner flame. Note: - (a) Flame color - (b) Smoke characteristics - (c) Odor (waft gently, do not inhale directly) - (d) Residue **Step 5 — Solubility Test.** Add a small piece of the sample to ~$5 \text{ mL}$ each of toluene, acetone, and phenol (separately) in test tubes. Observe after $5$ minutes at room temperature, then heat gently if no change is seen. **Step 6 — Chemical Test (for PET).** Reflux ~$0.5 \text{ g}$ PET with ~$10 \text{ mL}$ of $10\%$ alcoholic KOH for $15$ minutes. Cool and acidify with dilute HCl. A **white precipitate of terephthalic acid** confirms PET. **Step 7 — FTIR Recording (if available).** Record the IR spectrum of a thin film or KBr pellet. Match the major absorption peaks to the reference spectra of PE or PET. **Step 8 — Conclusion.** Compile all observations in a tabular format and compare with the standard reference matrix above to identify the polymer conclusively. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example > **Problem:** A polymer sample of mass $0.85 \text{ g}$ displaces $0.92 \text{ cm}^3$ of water when immersed. Identify whether it is PE or PET. **Solution:** $$\begin{aligned} \rho_{polymer} &= \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{0.85 \text{ g}}{0.92 \text{ cm}^3} \\ \rho_{polymer} &= 0.924 \text{ g/cm}^3 \end{aligned}$$ Comparing with reference values: - PE (LDPE): $0.910 - 0.925 \text{ g/cm}^3$ → **Matches!** - PET: $1.30 - 1.40 \text{ g/cm}^3$ → Does not match. **Conclusion:** The sample is **LDPE (Low-Density Polyethylene)**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Chemical Structure of PE and PET The following Mermaid diagram provides a structural-functional comparison of the repeat units and key functional groups. ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["ETHYLENE MONOMER
CH2 double bond CH2
Molecular Weight 28 g/mol"] nodeB["POLYETHYLENE REPEAT UNIT
Dash CH2 dash CH2 dash
Saturated aliphatic chain"] nodeC["KEY FEATURES OF PE
Only C and C single bonds
Only C and H atoms
Non polar backbone"] nodeD["ETHYLENE GLYCOL
HO dash CH2 dash CH2 dash OH"] nodeE["TEREPHTHALIC ACID
HOOC dash C6H4 dash COOH"] nodeF["PET REPEAT UNIT
Dash O dash CH2 dash CH2 dash O dash CO dash C6H4 dash CO dash
Contains Ester Linkage"] nodeG["KEY FEATURES OF PET
Contains Ester group
Contains Benzene ring
Contains Oxygen atoms"] nodeH["IDENTIFICATION TESTS
Density Test and Burning Test and Solubility Test and FTIR"] nodeI["PE FLOATS
Density less than 1 g per cm3"] nodeJ["PET SINKS

Only C-H stretches"] nodeL["FTIR PEAKS FOR PET

Ester and aromatic peaks"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeC --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeH --> nodeJ nodeH --> nodeK nodeH --> nodeL ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Identification Workflow ```mermaid graph TD step1["STEP 1: RECEIVE UNKNOWN SAMPLE
Record label, color, weight"] step2["STEP 2: VISUAL AND TACTILE TEST
Waxy feel or hard feel"] step3{"STEP 3: DENSITY TEST
Floats or Sinks"} step4["STEP 4: BURNING TEST
Flame color, odor, smoke"] step5["STEP 5: SOLUBILITY TEST
Toluene, acetone, phenol"] step6["STEP 6: CHEMICAL TEST
