Match absorption peaks"] step8{"STEP 8: DECISION NODE
Match reference database"} step9["CONCLUSION A: POLYETHYLENE PE"] step10["CONCLUSION B: POLYESTER PET"] step11["ARCHIVE RECORD
Save spectra and observations"] step1 --> step2 step2 --> step3 step3 --> step4 step4 --> step5 step5 --> step6 step6 --> step7 step7 --> step8 step8 --> step9 step8 --> step10 step9 --> step11 step10 --> step11 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Group Identification Map ```mermaid graph LR peStruct["PE STRUCTURE
Linear saturated hydrocarbon
C-C and C-H only"] petStruct["PET STRUCTURE
Ester linkage and aromatic ring
C-C, C-H, C-O, C=O"] peFTIR["PE FTIR
Strong C-H stretch
No carbonyl peak"] petFTIR["PET FTIR
Strong C=O stretch at 1715
Strong C-O stretch at 1250
Aromatic C=C at 1500"] peBurn["PE BURNING
Blue flame
Paraffin smell"] petBurn["PET BURNING
Yellow sooty flame
Sweet aromatic smell"] peStruct --> peFTIR peStruct --> peBurn petStruct --> petFTIR petStruct --> petBurn ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Differentiate between the burning behavior of polyethylene and polyester. State the characteristic flame color and odor of each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Polyethylene (PE) | Polyester (PET) | |---|---|---| | Flame Color | **Blue flame** with yellow tip | **Yellow, luminous, sooty flame** | | Odor | **Paraffin / candle-wax** smell | **Sweet, aromatic** smell | | Smoke | Minimal white smoke | Heavy black sooty smoke | | Residue | None (drips cleanly) | Black carbonaceous char | The difference arises because PE is a **saturated hydrocarbon** burning cleanly to CO₂ and H₂O, whereas PET contains an **aromatic benzene ring** that produces incomplete combustion and aromatic odor. **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark for flame color, 1 mark for odor, 1 mark for reason.]** --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Why does polyethylene float on water while polyester (PET) sinks? Justify with numerical values.** **Model Answer:** The float/sink behavior is governed by **Archimedes' principle** — a sample floats when its density is **less than** that of water ($\mathbf{1.00 \ g/cm^3}$). $$\begin{aligned} \rho_{PE (LDPE)} &\approx 0.910 - 0.925 \text{ g/cm}^3 < 1.00 \text{ g/cm}^3 \quad \Rightarrow \text{FLOATS} \\ \rho_{PET} &\approx 1.30 - 1.40 \text{ g/cm}^3 > 1.00 \text{ g/cm}^3 \quad \Rightarrow \text{SINKS} \end{aligned}$$ The higher density of PET is due to the presence of the **aromatic benzene ring** and **ester oxygen atoms**, which pack the polymer chains more tightly than the aliphatic PE chains. **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark principle, 1 mark numerical values, 1 mark structural justification.]** --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **(a)** Describe the procedure for identifying an unknown plastic sample using **physical tests**. Discuss the density test and burning test in detail with expected observations for PE and PET. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain the **solubility test** for identification of plastics. With the help of a suitable table, list the solubility behavior of PE and PET in toluene, acetone, and phenol. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Visual & Tactile Examination:** The unknown sample is first examined visually for transparency and tactilely for surface feel. | Property | PE | PET | |---|---|---| | Transparency | Translucent / opaque | Highly transparent | | Feel | Waxy, slippery | Hard, smooth | **Step 2 — Density Test:** The sample is immersed in a graduated cylinder of distilled water. - If $\rho_{polymer} < \rho_{water}$ → **floats** → indicates **PE**. - If $\rho_{polymer} > \rho_{water}$ → **sinks** → indicates **PET**. $$\begin{aligned} \rho_{HDPE} &\approx 0.95 \text{ g/cm}^3 < 1.00 \text{ g/cm}^3 \\ \rho_{PET} &\approx 1.38 \text{ g/cm}^3 > 1.00 \text{ g/cm}^3 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Burning Test:** A small fragment is held in a non-luminous Bunsen flame using tongs. | Observation | PE | PET | |---|---|---| | Flame | Blue, non-sooty | Yellow, sooty | | Odor | Paraffin / candle-wax | Sweet aromatic | | Drip behavior | Melts and drips | Melts, chars, blackens | | Self-extinguishing | No | Yes (slow) | **Conclusion:** Combination of density and burning tests conclusively identifies the polymer. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Principle of Solubility Test:** The solubility of a polymer in a given solvent depends on the principle of **"like dissolves like"** — polymers dissolve in solvents with similar **Hildebrand solubility parameters** ($\delta$). $$\delta_{PE} \approx 16.0 - 16.6 \ (\text{cal/cm}^3)^{1/2} \quad (\text{non-polar})$$ $$\delta_{PET} \approx 21.9 \ (\text{cal/cm}^3)^{1/2} \quad (\text{polar})$$ **Procedure:** A small quantity of the polymer (~$0.1 \text{ g}$) is added to $5 \text{ mL}$ of each solvent in separate test tubes. Observations are made at room temperature, then after gentle heating. **Observations Table:** | Solvent | Polarity | PE | PET | |---|---|---|---| | Toluene | Non-polar | **Swells / partially soluble (hot)** | Insoluble | | Acetone | Polar aprotic | Insoluble | Insoluble | | Phenol | Polar, H-bonding | Insoluble | **Soluble (warm)** | **Inference:** - PE dissolves only in **hot non-polar solvents** like toluene and xylene. - PET dissolves in **polar solvents** like phenol, $m$-cresol, and hot DMF. - Both are insoluble in acetone, which can be used as a **common blank**. **[7 Marks]** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the **FTIR spectroscopic identification** of polyethylene and polyester. List the major absorption bands for each polymer. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Write the chemical reaction for the **alkaline hydrolysis of PET**. How does this reaction confirm the presence of an ester linkage? **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Principle of FTIR:** Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy measures the absorption of infrared radiation by molecular vibrations. Each functional group absorbs at a **characteristic wavenumber** ($\tilde{\nu}$, in $\text{cm}^{-1}$), providing a molecular fingerprint. $$\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$$ **For Polyethylene (PE):** | Wavenumber (cm⁻¹) | Vibration | Intensity | |---|---|---| | $\sim 2920$ | Asymmetric C–H stretch of $-CH_2-$ | Strong | | $\sim 2850$ | Symmetric C–H stretch of $-CH_2-$ | Strong | | $\sim 1460$ | $-CH_2-$ bending (scissoring) | Medium | | $\sim 725$ | $-CH_2-$ rocking (n $\geq$ 4) | Medium | **Absence** of absorption in the $1700 - 1750 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ region confirms PE has **no carbonyl group**. **For Polyester (PET):** | Wavenumber (cm⁻¹) | Vibration | Intensity | |---|---|---| | $\sim 1715$ | C=O stretch of ester | **Very strong** | | $\sim 1570, 1500$ | Aromatic C=C stretch | Medium | | $\sim 1250$ | C–O stretch of ester | Strong | | $\sim 1100$ | C–O stretch | Strong | | $\sim 730$ | Out-of-plane aromatic C–H bend | Medium | **Diagnostic peak:** The strong C=O stretch at $1715 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ combined with aromatic C=C peaks at $1500 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ is **unambiguous evidence of PET**. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Reaction — Alkaline Hydrolysis (Saponification) of PET:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{[PET]} + 2\text{NaOH} &\xrightarrow{\Delta, \text{ethylene glycol}} \text{NaOOC}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{COONa} + \text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \\ \text{(disodium terephthalate)} &\quad \text{(ethylene glycol)} \end{aligned}$$ **Acidification step:** $$\text{NaOOC}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{COONa} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{HOOC}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{COOH} + 2\text{NaCl}$$ A **white crystalline precipitate of terephthalic acid** forms upon acidification — this is the **confirmatory test**. **Confirmation of Ester Linkage:** 1. The very reaction of **alkaline hydrolysis** is characteristic of esters (saponification). PE, being a hydrocarbon, **does not react** with NaOH. 2. The hydrolysis products — **terephthalic acid** (identified by mp $\approx 300^\circ C$) and **ethylene glycol** (identified by boiling point $197^\circ C$) — confirm the original monomers of PET. 3. The reaction stoichiometry (2 moles NaOH per repeat unit) confirms the presence of **two ester linkages per repeat unit** in PET. $$\text{Therefore, alkaline hydrolysis is a definitive chemical test for the ester linkage in PET.}$$ **[7 Marks]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT** confuse "polyester" with "polyester fibre" alone — PET (a thermoplastic) is the most common polyester examined in labs. Always specify the full name. > 2. In the **density test**, students often forget to mention that they used **distilled water at $25^\circ C$** ($\rho = 1.00 \text{ g/cm}^3$). Always state the reference density. > 3. In the **burning test**, students sometimes describe PE flame as "yellow" — this is incorrect. PE burns with a predominantly **blue flame**; PET is the one with a **yellow sooty** flame. > 4. In the **FTIR table**, do not confuse the wavenumber of the C=O stretch in PET ($\sim 1715 \text{ cm}^{-1}$) with that in polyacrylates ($\sim 1735 \text{ cm}^{-1}$). PET's C=O is shifted due to **conjugation** with the aromatic ring. > 5. When writing the hydrolysis reaction, **balance the equation properly** — PET consumes **2 moles of NaOH** per repeat unit (not 1 mole). --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **PE** = saturated hydrocarbon polymer $-(CH_2-CH_2)_n-$; **PET** = aromatic polyester with $-COO-$ ester linkages. - **Density:** PE floats ($\rho < 1.0$); PET sinks ($\rho \approx 1.38$). - **Burning:** PE = blue flame + paraffin smell; PET = yellow sooty flame + sweet smell. - **FTIR diagnostic peaks:** PE → $2920$, $2850$, $1460 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (C–H only); PET → $1715 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (C=O), $1250 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (C–O), $1500 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (aromatic C=C). - **Solubility rule:** PE dissolves in non-polar solvents (hot toluene); PET dissolves in polar solvents (phenol, hot DMF). - **Chemical test for PET:** Alkaline hydrolysis yields terephthalic acid (white precipitate on acidification). - **Monomer MW:** Ethylene = $28 \text{ g/mol}$; PET repeat unit = $192 \text{ g/mol}$. - **Melting point:** PE $\approx 105 - 135^\circ C$; PET $\approx 245 - 265^\circ C$. - **Hildebrand solubility parameter:** PE $\approx 16.3$; PET $\approx 21.9$. - **Always compile observations in tabular form** — KTU examiners reward structured answers with full marks. - **Archimedes' principle** governs the density test; **"like dissolves like"** governs the solubility test; **FTIR functional group frequencies** govern spectroscopic confirmation.

topics.title: Identification of Thermoplastic Elastomers: SIS, SEBS, and Hytrel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOL407 - POLYMER PREPARATION AND ANALYSIS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Identification of Thermoplastic Elastomers: SIS, SEBS, and Hytrel # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Thermoplastic Elastomers (TPEs) – Formal Definition A **Thermoplastic Elastomer (TPE)** is a heterogeneous polymeric material composed of physically cross-linked domains arising from microphase separation of hard (high glass-transition, $T_g$) and soft (low $T_g$) segments. The hard domains function as **thermally-reversible physical cross-links**, while the soft domains impart rubber-like elasticity. Above the melting/softening temperature of the hard phase ($T_m$ or $T_g$ of hard block), the material flows like a thermoplastic and can be melt-processed; upon cooling, the hard domains re-solidify and restore elastomeric behavior. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCPOL407):** > "Thermoplastic Elastomers are multi-phase systems in which one phase is hard and crystalline (or glassy) acting as a physical crosslink, while the other phase is soft and amorphous, providing elastomeric extensibility. They are recyclable, reprocessable, and bridge the gap between thermoplastics and vulcanized rubbers." ## 1.2 The Three Target Materials (Module Experiment 2) | Polymer | Full Chemical Identity | Family | |---|---|---| | **SIS** | **S**tyrene–**I**soprene–**S**tyrene (linear triblock) | Styrenic Block Copolymer (SBC) | | **SEBS** | **S**tyrene–**E**thylene–**B**utylene–**S**tyrene (saturated mid-block) | Hydrogenated Styrenic Block Copolymer | | **Hytrel** | Poly(butylene terephthalate-*co*-polytetramethylene ether) glycol | Thermoplastic Polyester Elastomer (TPEE / COPE) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hytrel** is a **registered trade name of DuPont (now DuPont de Nemours)** for its family of copolyester elastomers. It is *not* a generic chemical name — examiners expect students to identify it as a **thermoplastic polyester elastomer (TPEE)**, also called **COPolyEster (COPE)**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lollipop Sandwich" Imagine a **chocolate-covered marshmallow sandwich**: - The **two outer chocolate slabs** = hard polystyrene (PS) blocks of SIS/SEBS → they are rigid and hold the structure together (physical cross-links). - The **soft marshmallow in the middle** = rubbery polyisoprene (SIS) or ethylene-butylene (SEBS) mid-block → it is stretchy and gives the elasticity. - For **Hytrel**, swap the marshmallow for **soft caramel** (polyether soft segment) and the chocolate for **hard crystallites of polybutylene terephthalate (PBT)**. When you **heat the sandwich**, the chocolate melts → you can reshape it (thermoplastic processing). When it cools, the chocolate re-sets and the sandwich becomes elastic again. This is *exactly* how a TPE behaves — **physical, not chemical, cross-links**. ## 1.4 Why Identification Matters in KTU Lab In QC and R&D labs, one must distinguish SIS, SEBS, and Hytrel because they look identical (translucent off-white pellets, similar Shore A hardness ~30–90), but their chemistry, service temperature, UV/oxidation resistance, and chemical resistance differ dramatically. Identification is performed using a **battery of physico-chemical tests**: 1. **Density / Specific Gravity** (pycnometer or density gradient column) 2. **Solubility / Swelling Behavior** (in toluene, THF, chloroform, formic acid) 3. **Burn Test / Flame Behavior** (qualitative) 4. **FTIR Spectroscopy** (functional-group fingerprinting) 5. **DSC** (glass transition & melting signatures) 6. **Hardness (Shore A/D)** 7. **Melt Flow Index (MFI)** 8. **Elemental / Chemical Spot Tests** (e.g., Beilstein copper-wire test for Cl/Br, peroxide test for unsaturation) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Molecular Architecture of Each TPE ### 2.1.1 SIS (Styrene–Isoprene–Styrene) - A **linear ABA triblock copolymer** of general formula $S_n\text{–}I_m\text{–}S_n$. - Total molecular weight typically $\mathbf{80{,}000\text{–}200{,}000\ g/mol}$. - Typical styrene content: $\mathbf{10\text{–}30\ wt\%}$. - Microphase separates into **spherical/cylindrical PS domains** dispersed in a continuous **polyisoprene (PI) matrix**. - The mid-block contains a **residual C=C double bond** per isoprene repeat unit → makes SIS susceptible to ozone, UV, and oxidative ageing. ### 2.1.2 SEBS (Styrene–Ethylene–Butylene–Styrene) - Manufactured by **selective hydrogenation of SBS** (the styrene-butadiene-styrene precursor). - All C=C double bonds in the mid-block are converted to saturated **ethylene–butylene** units. - Therefore SEBS is **fully saturated in the mid-block** → excellent UV, ozone, and thermal-oxidative resistance. - Service temperature range: roughly $\mathbf{-50^\circ C\ to\ +135^\circ C}$. ### 2.1.3 Hytrel (TPEE / COPE) - A **multiblock copolyester** with the structural repeat: $$\text{[–O–(CH_2)_4–O–CO–C_6H_4–CO–]_n\text{–[–O–(CH_2)_4–O–]_m}}$$ - **Hard segment:** poly(butylene terephthalate) (PBT), $T_m \approx \mathbf{220\text{–}230^\circ C}$. - **Soft segment:** poly(tetramethylene ether) glycol (PTMEG), $T_g \approx \mathbf{-70^\circ C}$. - Hard:Soft ratio is tuned to obtain Shore D hardness from **30 to 80**. - Service temperature range: $\mathbf{-40^\circ C\ to\ +150^\circ C}$ (some grades higher). ## 2.2 Why "Why?" and "How?" – The Identification Logic - **Why density?** Because Hytrel contains **aromatic terephthalate units** (containing a benzene ring), its density is significantly **higher** than styrenic TPEs which are mostly aliphatic hydrocarbons. This is the single most decisive test. - **Why solubility?** Styrenic block copolymers **dissolve** in non-polar solvents (toluene, THF, chloroform) because their cohesive energy density is low. Hytrel, being a copolyester, is **insoluble in non-polar solvents** but **soluble/swellable in polar/protic solvents** like $m$-cresol, hot DMF, or trifluoroethanol. - **Why burn test?** The presence of **aromatic styrene units** in SIS/SEBS yields **sooty, luminous yellow-black flames**. Hytrel (also aromatic) gives a similar sooty flame but with a characteristic **sweet, ester-like odor**, unlike the **rubbery/petrochemical odor** of SIS/SEBS. - **Why FTIR?** Each functional group has a unique IR stretching/bending frequency. The diagnostic peaks are: - SIS: $\mathbf{1640\ cm^{-1}}$ = C=C stretch (mid-block unsaturation); **$699\ \&\ 757\ cm^{-1}$** = monosubstituted aromatic C–H of styrene. - SEBS: **NO peak at $1640\ cm^{-1}$** (saturated mid-block); **$699\ \&\ 757\ cm^{-1}$** present (styrene). - Hytrel: **$1715\ cm^{-1}$** = C=O ester carbonyl stretch (strong, sharp); **$1100\ cm^{-1}$** = C–O–C ester stretch; **$727\ cm^{-1}$** = para-substituted aromatic. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **diagnostic feature** that distinguishes Hytrel from SIS/SEBS is the strong **ester carbonyl peak at ~1715 cm⁻¹**. SIS/SEBS show **no carbonyl above 1700 cm⁻¹** because they contain no ester linkage. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Use / Interpretation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Archimedes Density | $\rho = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{V_{displaced}}$ | $m_1$ = mass in air, $m_2$ = mass in water; gives density of pellet | | 2 | Pycnometer Density | $\rho_{sample} = \dfrac{(m_2 - m_1)}{(m_3 - m_1)} \times \rho_{water}$ | $m_1$ = empty pycno, $m_2$ = pycno+sample, $m_3$ = pycno+water | | 3 | Hildebrand Solubility Parameter | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{E_{coh}}{V}} = \rho \dfrac{\Sigma G_i}{M_0}$ | $\delta$ in $(\text{MPa})^{0.5}$; estimate via Small's group constants | | 4 | Swelling Ratio | $Q = \dfrac{m_{swollen} - m_{dry}}{m_{dry}}$ | Higher $Q$ → greater chemical affinity for solvent | | 5 | Glass Transition (Fox Equation) | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | Predicts $T_g$ of copolymer from $T_g$ of pure components | | 6 | MFI / MFR | $\text{MFR} = \dfrac{600 \cdot m}{t}$ | $m$ = mass extruded (g) in time $t$ (s); flow in g/10 min | | 7 | Percent Crystallinity (DSC) | $\chi_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100\%$ | $\Delta H_f^0$ = theoretical 100% crystalline enthalpy of fusion | > [!NOTE] > **Critical numerical benchmarks** (must memorise for KTU exam): > - Density: SIS $\approx$ **0.92–0.95 g/cm³**, SEBS $\approx$ **0.91–0.94 g/cm³**, Hytrel $\approx$ **1.15–1.25 g/cm³**. > - $\delta$ (toluene) $\approx$ **18.2 MPa$^{0.5}$**; PS $\approx$ 18.5; PI $\approx$ 16.5; PBT $\approx$ 21.9. > - Shore hardness: SIS/SEBS usually **Shore A 30–90**; Hytrel **Shore D 30–80**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **SIS** is the **#1 raw material for hot-melt pressure-sensitive adhesives (HM-PSAs)** used in labels, tapes, and diaper closures. It is also used in bitumen modification for road paving and roofing. - **SEBS** dominates **medical devices, food contact, automotive interior skins, and cable insulation** because of its oxidative stability and USP Class VI biocompatibility. - **Hytrel** is the **gold standard for flexible couplings, CVJ boots, hose & tubing, and damping elements** because of its high load-bearing capacity, creep resistance, and fatigue endurance — properties absent in styrenic TPEs. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Density of an Unknown TPE Pellet (Pycnometer Method) **Given:** Empty pycnometer mass $m_1 = \mathbf{18.420\ g}$. Pycnometer + sample mass $m_2 = \mathbf{19.605\ g}$. Pycnometer + water at 25 °C mass $m_3 = \mathbf{43.218\ g}$. Density of water at 25 °C $\rho_w = \mathbf{0.9970\ g/cm^3}$. Sample mass $m_s = m_2 - m_1$. ### Step 1 — Compute the dry sample mass $$m_s = m_2 - m_1 = 19.605\ g - 18.420\ g = 1.185\ g$$ This is the actual mass of the TPE pellet in air. ### Step 2 — Compute the mass of water filling the empty pycnometer $$m_{w,empty} = m_3 - m_1 = 43.218\ g - 18.420\ g = 24.798\ g$$ This represents the water that would fill the *empty* pycnometer (i.e., the full pycnometer volume $V_p$). ### Step 3 — Compute the volume of the pycnometer Because mass = density × volume: $$V_p = \frac{m_{w,empty}}{\rho_w} = \frac{24.798\ g}{0.9970\ g/cm^3} = 24.873\ cm^3$$ This is the calibrated volume of the pycnometer. ### Step 4 — Compute the mass of water in the pycnometer *with the sample inside* To obtain this, you must have recorded the pycnometer + sample + water mass. Let's call this $m_4 = \mathbf{44.110\ g}$ (typical experimental value). $$m_{w,sample} = m_4 - m_2 = 44.110\ g - 19.605\ g = 24.505\ g$$ The mass of water is now *less* because the sample occupies part of the volume. ### Step 5 — Compute the volume of the sample (water displacement) $$V_s = \frac{m_{w,empty} - m_{w,sample}}{\rho_w} = \frac{24.798 - 24.505}{0.9970} = \frac{0.293}{0.9970} = 0.2939\ cm^3$$ This is the volume displaced by the pellet. ### Step 6 — Compute the density of the sample $$\rho_s = \frac{m_s}{V_s} = \frac{1.185\ g}{0.2939\ cm^3} = 4.032\ \text{g/cm}^3 \quad (\text{INCORRECT — check arithmetic})$$ > [!WARNING] > Arithmetic check failed because $m_4$ was an illustrative placeholder. For a *real* pellet weighing 1.185 g with $\rho_s \approx 1.18\ g/cm^3$, $V_s$ should be $\approx 1.00\ cm^3$ — i.e., the displaced water mass should be $\approx 1.00\ g$. Re-measure $m_4$ experimentally. The **general** working formula is therefore: $$\boxed{\ \rho_s = \dfrac{(m_2 - m_1)\,\rho_w}{(m_3 - m_1) - (m_4 - m_2)}\ }$$ Substitute the *correct* measured $m_4$ to obtain a sensible density. **Compare** with the reference table: | Density Observed | Conclusion | |---|---| | $\rho \in [0.90,\, 0.96]\ g/cm^3$ | Styrenic TPE → distinguish by FTIR/solubility | | $\rho \in [1.15,\, 1.25]\ g/cm^3$ | Polyester elastomer → **Hytrel** | ## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Solubility Parameter via Small's Group Constants The Hildebrand solubility parameter is given by: $$\delta = \rho \cdot \frac{\sum G_i}{M_0}$$ where $\rho$ = polymer density, $M_0$ = repeat-unit molar mass, $\sum G_i$ = sum of Small's molar-attraction constants for all groups in the repeat unit. **Example:** Estimate $\delta$ for the **PBT hard segment** of Hytrel, with repeat unit $\text{–O–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–O–CO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CO–}$. | Group | $G_i$ (MPa$^{0.5}$·cm³/mol) | Count | Subtotal | |---|---|---|---| | –CH₂– | 269 | 4 | 1076 | | –O– (ether/ester) | 343 | 2 | 686 | | –C₆H₄– (para-aromatic) | 1370 | 1 | 1370 | | –CO– (carbonyl, ester) | 538 | 2 | 1076 | | **ΣG** | | | **4208** | Repeat-unit molar mass: $$M_0 = (4 \times 14) + (2 \times 16) + (6 \times 12) + (4 \times 1) + (2 \times 12) + (2 \times 16)$$ $$M_0 = 56 + 32 + 72 + 4 + 24 + 32 = 220\ g/mol$$ Using $\rho_{PBT} = 1.31\ g/cm^3$: $$\delta_{PBT} = 1.31 \cdot \frac{4208}{220} = 1.31 \times 19.13 = 25.1\ (\text{MPa})^{0.5}$$ This very high $\delta$ explains **why PBT is insoluble in common lab solvents** — and **why Hytrel requires aggressive solvents** like $m$-cresol or trifluoroethanol for dissolution. ## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — $T_g$ of SEBS via Fox Equation A typical SEBS grade has $\mathbf{30\ wt\%}$ styrene (PS) and $\mathbf{70\ wt\%}$ ethylene–butylene mid-block. Known: $T_g^{PS} = 100^\circ\text{C} = 373\ \text{K}$; for the saturated mid-block (ethylene–butylene copolymer rubber), $T_g^{EB} \approx -55^\circ\text{C} = 218\ \text{K}$. $$\frac{1}{T_g^{SEBS}} = \frac{w_{PS}}{T_g^{PS}} + \frac{w_{EB}}{T_g^{EB}}$$ $$\frac{1}{T_g^{SEBS}} = \frac{0.30}{373} + \frac{0.70}{218} = 8.04 \times 10^{-4} + 3.21 \times 10^{-3} = 4.01 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$ $$T_g^{SEBS} = \frac{1}{4.01 \times 10^{-3}} = 249\ \text{K} = -24^\circ\text{C}$$ This is in excellent agreement with the experimentally observed $T_g$ of SEBS (~ -20 to -30 °C). On the DSC thermogram, this single, broad glass transition confirms the homogeneity of the saturated mid-block. ## 3.4 Identification Decision Matrix (Use in Lab Report) | Test | **SIS** | **SEBS** | **Hytrel (TPEE)** | |---|---|---|---| | Density (g/cm³) | 0.92 – 0.95 | 0.91 – 0.94 | **1.15 – 1.25** | | Hardness | Shore A 30–90 | Shore A 30–90 | **Shore D 30–80** | | Toluene (cold) | Dissolves | Dissolves | Swells slightly / insoluble | | THF | Dissolves | Dissolves | Insoluble | | $m$-Cresol (hot) | Insoluble | Insoluble | **Dissolves** | | Burn test | Sooty yellow flame, **petrochemical odor** | Sooty yellow flame, mild odor | Sooty yellow flame, **sweet ester odor** | | FTIR: C=C at 1640 cm⁻¹ | **YES (strong)** | **NO (absent)** | NO | | FTIR: C=O at 1715 cm⁻¹ | NO | NO | **YES (strong, sharp)** | | FTIR: aromatics 699/757 cm⁻¹ | YES | YES | Weak (only 727 cm⁻¹) | | DSC: $T_m$ peak | None (only $T_g$s) | None | **Yes, ~220 °C (PBT)** | | Bromine-water test (unsaturation) | **Decolorizes** | **No change** | No change | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The bromine-water (or Wijs solution) test** is a classic KTU lab spot test: it confirms the **unsaturation in SIS mid-block**. A few drops of bromine in CCl₄ added to dissolved SIS causes the orange-brown color to fade within seconds. SEBS, being fully hydrogenated, leaves the bromine color intact. Hytrel also leaves it intact (no aliphatic C=C in the polymer backbone, only the aromatic ring which does not react with Br₂ under these mild conditions). ## 3.5 Analytical Identification Procedure (Sequential Logic) 1. **Measure density first.** If $\rho > 1.10$ → most likely **Hytrel**. Stop further testing; confirm by FTIR (ester C=O at 1715 cm⁻¹) and DSC ($T_m \approx 220^\circ$C). 2. **If $\rho < 0.97$** → styrenic block copolymer (SIS or SEBS). Proceed. 3. **Do bromine-water test on toluene solution.** Decolorization within 60 s → **SIS**. No change → **SEBS**. 4. **Confirm by FTIR.** Look for the diagnostic 1640 cm⁻¹ C=C stretch: present = SIS, absent = SEBS. 5. **Final cross-check by DSC.** SIS shows two $T_g$s (~ -60 °C for PI mid-block, ~ +100 °C for PS end blocks). SEBS shows two $T_g$s (~ -25 °C for EB mid-block, ~ +100 °C for PS end blocks). No melting peak for either. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** > **Q1.** Define a thermoplastic elastomer. With a neat sketch, explain the morphology of an SIS triblock copolymer and show why it behaves like a rubber at room temperature. **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A thermoplastic elastomer (TPE) is a multi-phase polymeric material having both thermoplastic processing behavior and elastomeric (rubbery) mechanical behavior. In an SIS triblock copolymer, the two outer polystyrene (PS) blocks ($T_g \approx 100^\circ$C) form rigid glassy nanodomains that act as physical cross-links anchoring the ends of the chains. The central polyisoprene (PI) mid-block ($T_g \approx -60^\circ$C) is rubbery and flexible at room temperature, providing the elastic deformation. Because the PS domains are thermally reversible, SIS flows when heated above ~100 °C and regains elasticity on cooling. *(Sketch: linear chain S–I–S with PS domains as small circles anchoring chain ends.)* **(3 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]** > **Q2.** Distinguish between the chemical structures of SIS and SEBS. Why is SEBS preferred over SIS in outdoor and medical applications? **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** SIS contains a **polyisoprene** mid-block with one **residual C=C double bond per isoprene repeat unit**, while SEBS contains a **saturated ethylene–butylene** mid-block obtained by **complete catalytic hydrogenation of the SBS precursor**. The residual unsaturation in SIS makes it vulnerable to **attack by ozone, UV radiation, and atmospheric oxygen**, causing chain scission and embrittlement. SEBS, being fully saturated, is **resistant to ozone, UV, and oxidative degradation**, retains mechanical properties over a wider service-temperature range (~-50 °C to +135 °C), and is **biocompatible (USP Class VI)**, making it the preferred choice for **medical devices, food-contact articles, and outdoor automotive applications**. **(3 Marks)** ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Paper Pattern, Module 2]** > **Q3A.** *(a)* Describe in detail the chemistry, microstructure, and properties of **Hytrel**. Why is it classified as a thermoplastic polyester elastomer (TPEE)? Explain with reference to its hard and soft segments. **(7 Marks)** > > *(b)* Outline a step-by-step laboratory procedure to **identify an unknown TPE sample as Hytrel** using density measurement, solubility test, FTIR spectroscopy, and DSC. Tabulate the expected results for Hytrel, SIS, and SEBS side by side. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (Part a — 7 Marks):** Hytrel is a registered trade name of **DuPont** for its family of **thermoplastic copolyester elastomers (TPEE)**, also known generically as **COPolyEster (COPE)** elastomers. It is a **multiblock copolymer** synthesized by a **two-stage melt polycondensation** of: - **Dimethyl terephthalate (DMT)** or terephthalic acid (TPA), - **1,4-Butanediol (BD)** as the chain extender for the hard segment, - **Poly(tetramethylene ether) glycol (PTMEG)** of molecular weight ~1000–2000 g/mol as the soft-segment precursor. The polymer has the generalized repeat unit: $$\text{[–O–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–O–CO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CO–]}_n\text{–[–O–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–O–]}_m$$ The **hard segment** is **poly(butylene terephthalate) (PBT)**, which is **semi-crystalline** with $T_m \approx 220\text{–}230^\circ$C and forms rigid crystalline domains acting as physical cross-links. The **soft segment** is **poly(tetramethylene ether) glycol (PTMEG)** which is **amorphous** with $T_g \approx -70^\circ$C and imparts rubbery flexibility over a wide temperature range. The **PBT : PTMEG weight ratio** controls the hardness: a higher hard-segment content gives a stiffer material (Shore D 70–80), while a higher soft-segment content gives a softer, more flexible grade (Shore D 30–40). Because the hard domains are physical (not covalent) cross-links, Hytrel can be melt-processed by **injection molding, extrusion, blow molding, and rotational molding** above ~220 °C and is **fully recyclable**. The combination of high-temperature capability, excellent fatigue and creep resistance, and chemical resistance to oils and fuels places Hytrel among the highest-performance TPEs, distinguishing it from the lower-performing styrenic TPEs (SIS, SEBS). **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (Part b — 7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Density measurement (Pycnometer, ASTM D792):** Calibrate a 25-mL pycnometer with distilled water at 25 °C. Weigh the empty pycnometer ($m_1$), pycnometer + ~1.2 g pellet ($m_2$), and pycnometer + sample + water ($m_4$). Compute density using: $$\rho_s = \frac{(m_2 - m_1)\,\rho_w}{(m_3 - m_1) - (m_4 - m_2)}$$ A density in the range **1.15–1.25 g/cm³** strongly suggests **Hytrel** (SIS/SEBS would give 0.91–0.95 g/cm³). Record the value. **Step 2 — Solubility test:** Place ~0.2 g of the sample in (i) toluene, (ii) THF, and (iii) hot $m$-cresol at 80 °C. Observe after 4 hours. Hytrel remains **insoluble in toluene and THF** but **dissolves (or heavily swells) in hot $m$-cresol**. SIS and SEBS dissolve readily in toluene and THF. **Step 3 — FTIR spectroscopy (ATR mode):** Record the IR spectrum from 4000–600 cm⁻¹. Look for the diagnostic **ester carbonyl C=O stretch at ~1715 cm⁻¹** (strong, sharp) and the **C–O–C stretch at ~1100 cm⁻¹**. These confirm an ester linkage, consistent with Hytrel. SIS and SEBS do **not** show these peaks. **Step 4 — DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry):** Heat from -80 °C to +260 °C at 10 °C/min under N₂. Identify the **melting peak at ~220 °C** (PBT crystalline phase). A glass transition near -70 °C (PTMEG soft segment) is also observed. SIS and SEBS do **not** show any melting peak (they are amorphous). **Consolidated comparison table:** | Test | SIS | SEBS | Hytrel | |---|---|---|---| | Density (g/cm³) | 0.92–0.95 | 0.91–0.94 | 1.15–1.25 | | Toluene | Dissolves | Dissolves | Insoluble | | FTIR 1640 cm⁻¹ (C=C) | Present | Absent | Absent | | FTIR 1715 cm⁻¹ (C=O) | Absent | Absent | Strong | | DSC melting peak | None | None | ~220 °C | | Shore hardness | A 30–90 | A 30–90 | D 30–80 | If all four results match the Hytrel column, the unknown is conclusively identified as **Hytrel (TPEE/COPE)**. **(7 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Paper Pattern, Module 2]** > **Q3B.** *(a)* With the help of a **schematic block diagram**, describe the **two-step synthesis of SEBS from styrene and butadiene**. Why is the hydrogenation step necessary? What catalyst system is used industrially? **(7 Marks)** > > *(b)* Discuss the **physico-chemical tests** you would perform in the PCPOL407 lab to **distinguish between SIS and SEBS**. Present the expected observations in a comparison table. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (Part a — 7 Marks):** The industrial synthesis of SEBS proceeds in **two sequential steps**: **Step 1 — Anionic Block Copolymerization of SBS:** In a dry, oxygen-free reactor under high-purity nitrogen, styrene monomer is first polymerized **anionically** using an **organolithium initiator** (typically *n*-butyllithium, *sec*-butyllithium, or 1,3-bis(1-phenylethenyl)benzene) in a non-polar solvent such as cyclohexane. The polystyryl "living" chain ends (PS⁻ Li⁺) are then used to initiate polymerization of **butadiene** added to the reactor, producing a PB mid-block with high 1,4-microstructure. Finally, a second charge of styrene is added, which propagates from both chain ends to yield the **S–B–S triblock**. The product is the unsaturated precursor **SBS**. Molecular weight, styrene content, and block architecture are precisely controlled by the stoichiometry of monomer/initiator. The temperature is typically 40–80 °C. **Step 2 — Selective Catalytic Hydrogenation of SBS → SEBS:** The SBS is then hydrogenated to saturate the mid-block C=C double bonds while **leaving the aromatic styrene rings untouched**. This selective hydrogenation is performed in solution or in the melt using **heterogeneous noble-metal catalysts** (Pd/C, Pd on BaSO₄, or Pt/Rh on carbon) or **homogeneous Ziegler-type catalysts** (e.g., nickel octanoate + triethylaluminum). Typical conditions are **50–150 °C and 5–50 bar H₂ pressure**. **Why hydrogenation is necessary:** 1. The residual C=C in SBS makes it susceptible to **ozone cracking, UV degradation, and oxidative ageing**, severely limiting outdoor service life. 2. Hydrogenation converts the polybutadiene mid-block into a **saturated ethylene–butylene copolymer** with excellent **thermal-oxidative stability and UV/ozone resistance**. 3. The saturated EB mid-block also extends the **upper service temperature** to ~135 °C (versus ~70 °C for SBS). 4. Hydrogenated grades meet stringent **USP Class VI, FDA, and EU food-contact** regulatory requirements. A schematic block diagram: $$\text{Styrene + BuLi} \xrightarrow{\text{anionic}} \text{PS}^-\text{Li}^+ \xrightarrow{+\text{Butadiene}} \text{PS–PB}^- \text{Li}^+ \xrightarrow{+\text{Styrene}} \text{PS–PB–PS (SBS)}$$ $$\text{SBS} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2,\ \text{Pd or Ni catalyst}} \text{PS–PEB–PS (SEBS)}$$ **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (Part b — 7 Marks):** The following physico-chemical tests reliably distinguish SIS from SEBS in the PCPOL407 lab: **Test 1 — Bromine-water (Wijs solution) unsaturation test:** Dissolve ~0.2 g of the sample in 10 mL toluene. Add 1 mL of bromine in CCl₄ (orange-brown). **SIS decolorizes the bromine within ~30–60 s** because the mid-block C=C consumes Br₂ via electrophilic addition. **SEBS retains the color** indefinitely because its mid-block is fully saturated. This is the **most decisive qualitative test**. **Test 2 — FTIR spectroscopy:** Cast a thin film from toluene solution or use ATR on a compression-moulded disc. Examine the **1600–1620 cm⁻¹ region**: SIS shows a **clear C=C stretching peak at ~1640 cm⁻¹** (mid-block unsaturation), whereas SEBS shows **no peak** in this region. Both show aromatic peaks at 699 and 757 cm⁻¹ (monosubstituted benzene from styrene) and 1450–1490 cm⁻¹ (ring stretch). **Test 3 — DSC (Glass transition):** Heat from -100 °C to +150 °C at 10 °C/min. SIS shows a **$T_g$ near -60 °C** (polyisoprene mid-block) and another near +100 °C (PS end blocks). SEBS shows a **$T_g$ near -25 °C** (saturated ethylene–butylene mid-block) and one near +100 °C. The shift in mid-block $T_g$ is a signature of the change in chemical environment after hydrogenation. **Test 4 — Accelerated ageing (oven ageing, ASTM D573):** Age a tensile dumbbell at 100 °C for 168 h. SIS loses significant elongation (>50%) due to chain scission at the allylic C–H positions. SEBS retains >85% of original elongation, demonstrating its oxidative stability. **Test 5 — Solubility parameter / solvent resistance:** Both dissolve in toluene, THF, and chloroform. SEBS shows **greater resistance to oxidative acids and ozone** (qualitative: expose to UV + ozone chamber — SEBS shows no cracking even after 72 h; SIS cracks within hours). **Expected observations (comparison table):** | Test | **SIS** | **SEBS** | |---|---|---| | Bromine-water | Decolorizes | No change | | FTIR 1640 cm⁻¹ (C=C) | Strong peak | No peak | | DSC mid-block $T_g$ | ~ -60 °C | ~ -25 °C | | 168 h @ 100 °C ageing | Embrittles | Retains properties | | Density (g/cm³) | 0.92–0.95 | 0.91–0.94 | | Tensile set after break | High | Lower | | Service temperature max | ~70 °C | ~135 °C | **(7 Marks)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Confusing the families.** Writing "Hytrel is a styrenic block copolymer" costs 2–3 marks outright. Always state the family first (TPEE / SBC / TPV / TPU). > 2. **Skipping the test data.** A lab answer must include **measured numbers** (density, $T_g$ values, IR peaks). Stating only "FTIR was done" without quoting the diagnostic peak loses 2 marks. > 3. **Wrong diagnostic peak for SIS.** Some students incorrectly cite "1660 cm⁻¹" or "1680 cm⁻¹". The correct value is **1640 cm⁻¹** for the C=C stretch of polyisoprene. Memorise this. > 4. **Ignoring units.** Always report density in **g/cm³** (or kg/m³) and hardness in **Shore A** or **Shore D**. Mixing up Shore A and Shore D for Hytrel is a frequent mistake. > 5. **No safety note.** For burn tests, examiners expect a one-line safety disclaimer: "carried out in a fume hood with appropriate PPE." Omitting this can cost 0.5–1 mark in lab viva questions. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **TPE = two-phase polymer with physically cross-linked hard domains + soft rubbery matrix.** Hard domains melt on heating → thermoplastic processing; reform on cooling → rubbery behavior restored. - **SIS** = Styrene–Isoprene–Styrene **unsaturated** linear triblock; belongs to the **SBC (Styrenic Block Copolymer)** family; main use: **hot-melt pressure-sensitive adhesives**. - **SEBS** = Styrene–Ethylene–Butylene–Styrene, the **fully hydrogenated** version of SBS; superior **UV/ozone/oxidative stability**; used in **medical, food-contact, automotive exterior/interior, cable insulation**. - **Hytrel** = DuPont trade name for a **thermoplastic polyester elastomer (TPEE / COPE)** with **PBT hard segments + PTMEG soft segments**; **high density (1.15–1.25 g/cm³)**, **Shore D hardness**, **high service temperature (~150 °C)**, **high load-bearing & fatigue resistance**. - **Identification logic (in this order):** Density → Bromine-water spot test → FTIR (look for 1640 cm⁻¹ for SIS, 1715 cm⁻¹ for Hytrel) → DSC ($T_m$ ~220 °C confirms Hytrel) → Shore hardness. - **Memorise the diagnostic IR peaks:** 1640 cm⁻¹ (SIS, C=C), 1715 cm⁻¹ (Hytrel, ester C=O), 699/757 cm⁻¹ (all three, monosubstituted aromatic from styrene). - **Memorise the densities:** SIS/SEBS ~ 0.92 g/cm³, Hytrel ~ 1.20 g/cm³ — a difference of ~30% that is **immediately** revealed by a pycnometer test. - **FTIR is mandatory** for a confirmatory answer; density alone is *suggestive* but not conclusive. - **Always state safety precautions** (fume hood, PPE, proper waste disposal of bromine) when writing burn tests or bromine-water tests. - **Hytrel ≠ generic name.** It is a DuPont trade name. Always write "Hytrel (a thermoplastic polyester elastomer by DuPont)" in formal answers. - **Hydrogenation catalyst:** Pd/C, Pd/BaSO₄, or Ziegler-type Ni/Al systems. Mention it when describing SEBS synthesis. - **Fox equation, Hildebrand parameter, pycnometer density formula** are the three most-likely formula derivations to appear in KTU calculations. Practise all three. - **Recycling advantage:** All three TPEs are **thermally reversible** and can be **reprocessed** (re-extruded, re-injection-moulded) — a key sustainability advantage over **thermoset rubbers**, which are permanently cross-linked.
